vignette: Inés Lobato, Head of People at a Madrid logistics company with 380 employees, has completed the firm's first salary audit following the transposition of the Pay Transparency Directive. The audit reveals a 9% unexplained gender pay gap concentrated in the operations division, affecting 42 women. Inés must propose a remediation plan to the board.

options.A: Commission a six-month external diversity-training programme for the operations division's management team.
options.B: Defer the remediation to the next fiscal year to spread the cost over two budget cycles, reporting the gap in the annual sustainability disclosure as 'under review'.
options.C: Build a 24-month banded remediation plan with quarterly salary adjustments, individual notification to affected employees, and quarterly disclosure of progress to the works council, in line with the Pay Transparency Directive's reporting obligations.
options.D: Issue a company-wide statement reaffirming the firm's commitment to fair pay and equal opportunity.

vignette: Pieter Vandenberghe, HR Director at a Belgian engineering consultancy with 220 employees, learns that fewer than 25% of female engineers return after parental leave, against a male return rate of 91%. Exit interviews cite rigid scheduling and lack of childcare arrangements during off-hours work peaks.

options.A: Launch a STEM mentorship programme targeting female university students in the consultancy's recruitment pipeline.
options.B: Implement the full set of flexible-working entitlements under the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158, including a four-month non-transferable parental leave per parent and a guaranteed right to request flexible arrangements upon return, and partner with two local crèches to subsidise off-hours childcare.
options.C: Maintain the current scheduling policy and document the attrition pattern in the annual people report, citing market norms in the engineering sector.
options.D: Add a 'family-friendly employer' badge to the recruitment website and feature returning mothers in the next internal newsletter.

vignette: Małgorzata Wójcik, Compensation Lead at a Warsaw retail chain with 1,200 employees, discovers during the Pay Transparency Directive readiness review that job postings do not currently disclose salary ranges, and that pre-employment salary history is collected as part of the standard onboarding form.

options.A: Publish a press release announcing the company's commitment to pay transparency in advance of the directive's deadline.
options.B: Launch an unconscious-bias training module for hiring managers, leaving the application form unchanged.
options.C: Disclose ranges only for vacancies above the median compensation band, on the grounds that disclosure for entry-level roles would compress recruitment flexibility.
options.D: Mandate disclosure of indicative salary ranges in every external job posting and remove the salary-history field from the application form, in line with the Pay Transparency Directive's pre-employment transparency provisions.

vignette: Henri Lefèvre, Operations Manager at a Lyon manufacturing plant with 540 employees, reviews the gender breakdown of overtime allocation. Men receive 78% of overtime hours, despite women constituting 41% of the eligible workforce. The pattern is undocumented and largely informal.

options.A: Reorganise shift patterns to improve overall plant productivity, without specifically tracking the gender dimension of overtime.
options.B: Introduce a documented, gender-neutral overtime allocation rota with quarterly audit of distribution by gender, and report findings to the works council, addressing the structural overtime-pay disparity that contributes to the 15.7% EU gender pay gap.
options.C: Maintain informal allocation but cap total overtime hours per quarter to reduce overall cost exposure, deferring any reform to the next collective bargaining round.
options.D: Add a line in the employee handbook stating that overtime is allocated on an equal-opportunity basis.

vignette: Sofia Zacharopoulou, People Operations Lead at an Athens fintech with 95 employees, finds that the bonus-pool allocation last year favoured men by 22 percentage points despite comparable performance scores. The bonus committee operates without documented criteria.

options.A: Apply the rubric to next year's allocations only, treating the historical disparity as out of scope for budgetary reasons.
options.B: Adopt a written bonus-allocation rubric tied to documented performance metrics, audit historical allocations against the rubric, and remediate the prior-year shortfall through a one-off correction to affected employees, consistent with the equal-pay-for-work-of-equal-value principle of Treaty Article 157 TFEU.
options.C: Replace the current performance-management software with a vendor that markets itself as bias-aware.
options.D: Introduce a 'fairness in compensation' annual statement signed by the executive committee.

vignette: Caoimhe O'Brien, HR Lead at a Dublin software firm with 280 employees, notices that female-led teams systematically receive smaller annual budget allocations than male-led teams of comparable scope. Internal review confirms the pattern persists across three fiscal years.

options.A: Promote a high-profile woman to a senior leadership role and publicise the appointment in the trade press.
options.B: Freeze all team budgets at current levels for the next year to avoid distributional disputes during a tight fiscal period.
options.C: Restructure the budget-allocation process to anchor allocations on documented team-scope metrics, conduct a corrective top-up for under-resourced female-led teams, and publish allocation methodology in the annual sustainability disclosure.
options.D: Commission an external study on the gender composition of the firm's customer base.

vignette: Tobias Krüger, Head of Compensation at a Hamburg shipping company with 720 employees, finds during a job-classification review that several traditionally female-dominated administrative roles are graded one band lower than equivalently-skilled traditionally male-dominated technical roles, despite comparable competence requirements.

options.A: Issue an internal memo affirming the company's principle of equal pay for work of equal value.
options.B: Re-evaluate the affected roles using a gender-neutral job-evaluation methodology, regrade the under-classified positions with retroactive salary adjustment, and document the methodology in the company's pay-transparency report under the Pay Transparency Directive (EU) 2023/970.
options.C: Acknowledge the discrepancy in the next collective bargaining round and absorb adjustments gradually over five years.
options.D: Launch a leadership-development programme for high-potential women across all bands.

vignette: Anneli Virtanen, People Director at a Helsinki insurance firm with 410 employees, observes that the company offers no return-to-work onboarding for employees coming back from parental leave, and that 60% of returners report they were assigned diminished portfolios on return, predominantly women.

options.A: Send a personalised welcome-back card to each returner, signed by the CEO.
options.B: Document the issue but defer programme design to the next budget cycle, given competing priorities in the digital-transformation roadmap.
options.C: Establish a formal four-week return-to-work onboarding programme with portfolio-restoration guarantees, in line with the Work-Life Balance Directive's protection-against-detriment provisions, and audit returner-portfolio outcomes annually.
options.D: Subscribe the company to a generic LinkedIn Learning licence for all employees.

vignette: Ricardo Mendes, HR Manager at a Lisbon tourism operator with 160 seasonal employees, finds that female seasonal workers are systematically offered shorter contracts than men in equivalent roles, with the consequence that women rarely qualify for the firm's permanent-staff conversion track.

options.A: Invest in customer-experience training for all front-line seasonal staff.
options.B: Run an annual 'women in tourism' awareness day featuring talks from senior female staff.
options.C: Maintain the current contract-issuance practice but introduce a discretionary review mechanism for cases flagged by employees themselves.
options.D: Equalise contract durations across the seasonal workforce on a documented role-based basis, audit the conversion-track pipeline annually for gender balance, and align the contract-issuance process with the Council 2019 conclusions on closing the gender pay gap.

vignette: Klara Janssens, Head of Total Rewards at a Brussels professional-services firm with 880 employees, learns that the year-end equity-grant pool has historically been distributed with a 65/35 split favouring men, despite comparable role distributions, and that no allocation criteria are documented.

options.A: Introduce a cohort-based mentorship scheme matching senior partners with mid-career associates.
options.B: Apply the new criteria prospectively only, citing the impracticality of recalculating historical grants under the existing share-plan terms.
options.C: Issue documented equity-allocation criteria tied to role and tenure, conduct a one-off corrective grant to historically under-allocated employees, and report grant distribution by gender in the annual gender-pay-gap disclosure required under the Pay Transparency Directive.
options.D: Add a 'pay equity' page to the firm's recruitment site, listing the firm's commitments.

vignette: Damir Kovač, Compensation Manager at a Zagreb energy utility with 1,500 employees, identifies during the EU pay-transparency readiness review that performance ratings show a 0.4-point gender gap on a 5-point scale that is unexplained by output metrics, and that ratings drive 40% of base-salary growth.

options.A: Tighten rating documentation requirements going forward, treating the historical gap as a training-effect issue not requiring corrective adjustment.
options.B: Audit the rating distribution against documented output metrics, recalibrate ratings showing unexplained gender variance, and embed gender-distribution review into the annual rating-cycle governance, in line with the Pay Transparency Directive's joint-pay-assessment obligation.
options.C: Replace the rating system with a continuous-feedback platform that does not anchor on numerical scores.
options.D: Roll out a one-hour 'fair appraisal' webinar to all line managers.

vignette: Beata Nowak, HR Manager at a Kraków manufacturing plant with 320 employees, finds that the on-site canteen and uniform-supply policies disproportionately disadvantage women — for example, no maternity-uniform sizing exists, and pregnancy accommodations are negotiated case-by-case with no documented standard.

options.A: Continue the case-by-case approach but designate one HR contact as the 'pregnancy liaison' to streamline future negotiations.
options.B: Upgrade the canteen menu to include healthier options for all employees.
options.C: Issue a documented pregnancy-and-postnatal accommodations standard covering uniforms, ergonomic adjustments, and break entitlements, in alignment with the Maternity Leave Directive 92/85/EEC, and integrate the standard into the new-hire onboarding pack.
options.D: Display a 'we support working mothers' poster in the canteen and break rooms.

vignette: Luc Dupont, Director of Talent at a Paris advertising agency with 240 employees, reviews the agency's freelance-engagement records and finds that female freelancers are paid on average 14% less than male freelancers for equivalent project briefs, with no documented rationale.

options.A: Apply the rate card to new engagements only, on the grounds that retrospective adjustments would breach existing contract terms.
options.B: Add a clause to the freelance-engagement template stating that the firm pays equitably regardless of gender.
options.C: Convene a 'creative women' panel at the next industry conference, featuring three of the firm's female freelancers.
options.D: Establish a documented freelance-rate card tied to deliverable type and seniority band, retroactively review and equalise rates on active engagements, and disclose freelance-spend distribution by gender in the firm's pay-transparency report.

vignette: Eleni Konstantinou, Head of Reward at a Thessaloniki shipping firm with 510 employees, finds that the annual sales-commission scheme disproportionately rewards account types historically held by male sales staff, and that the 70/30 commission split has remained unchanged for eight years despite shifts in the team's gender composition.

options.A: Recalibrate the commission scheme on the basis of a documented account-difficulty rubric, ensure equivalent-effort accounts attract equivalent commission rates, and review distributional outcomes annually under the company's Pay Transparency Directive joint-assessment process.
options.B: Run a women-in-sales recognition awards ceremony at the next national sales kickoff.
options.C: Defer scheme redesign to the next strategic-plan cycle to avoid mid-year disruption to commercial operations.
options.D: Introduce a CRM upgrade to improve account-tracking accuracy across the sales team.

vignette: Jorge Ribeiro, People Lead at a Porto biotech with 75 employees, learns that the company offers no formal childcare support, even though the local Barcelona-target shortfall is well documented, and that two senior female scientists have given resignation notice citing childcare unaffordability.

options.A: Introduce an employer-subsidised childcare benefit (vouchers or partner-crèche placement), aligned with the revised Barcelona targets for early childhood education and care, and document the benefit alongside parental-leave entitlements in the employee handbook.
options.B: Acknowledge the issue in exit interviews and revisit the benefit design when the next funding round is closed.
options.C: Highlight the firm's family-friendly values on the recruitment website and in employer-branding campaigns.
options.D: Establish a women-in-science speaker series featuring senior scientists from partner universities.

vignette: Sara Lindqvist, HR Director at a Stockholm food retailer with 2,400 employees, reviews part-time staffing patterns and finds that 78% of part-time staff are women, with no documented progression path from part-time to full-time, and a notable absence of part-time workers in management roles.

options.A: Promote a high-profile part-time manager and feature her trajectory in an internal newsletter.
options.B: Establish a documented part-time-to-full-time conversion track with annual review milestones, ensure part-time roles are explicitly eligible for management positions, and report the conversion-track outcomes by gender in the annual sustainability disclosure.
options.C: Roll out a customer-service improvement programme across all retail outlets.
options.D: Offer a small loyalty bonus to long-tenure part-time staff while maintaining current management-eligibility criteria.

vignette: Rasmus Holm, Compensation Analyst at a Copenhagen bank with 1,100 employees, finds during the Pay Transparency Directive readiness analysis that mortgage-approval discretion exercised by relationship managers has produced a 7% gender disparity in customer mortgage rates — a pattern with both customer-facing and reputational compliance implications.

options.A: Tighten the discretionary-pricing framework with documented criteria, audit historical pricing decisions for gender disparity, and report findings to the bank's compliance committee in line with the Equal Treatment in Goods and Services Directive 2004/113/EC.
options.B: Update the bank's customer-charter brochure to include a fair-treatment commitment.
options.C: Maintain manager discretion but introduce post-decision sampling reviews on a quarterly basis.
options.D: Roll out a unified customer-relationship-management platform to standardise data capture across branches.

vignette: Daniela Popescu, HR Manager at a Bucharest IT services firm with 350 employees, observes that women receive on average two fewer training days per year than men, and that the firm's training-allocation process is informal — managers nominate participants without documented criteria.

options.A: Introduce a documented training-allocation procedure tied to development plans, ring-fence equal training-day budgets per employee, and audit annual distribution by gender in the firm's people-strategy report.
options.B: Launch a 'continuous learning' brand campaign internally and on the recruitment site.
options.C: Maintain the current allocation process but cap manager nominations at a flat per-team budget for the next fiscal year.
options.D: Migrate all training content to a new e-learning platform with automated certification tracking.

vignette: Jakob Müller, Head of People at a Vienna logistics SME with 120 employees, finds that the firm's overtime-pay policy excludes part-time workers (predominantly women), even when their hours exceed contracted thresholds — an arrangement that has not been reviewed since the firm was founded.

options.A: Extend overtime-pay eligibility to all workers exceeding contracted hours regardless of contract type, in line with the Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC and the equal-pay-for-equal-work principle, and back-pay affected employees over the past 24 months.
options.B: Apply the new policy prospectively only, citing the operational complexity of historical recalculation.
options.C: Publish the firm's commitment to part-time worker fairness in the next sustainability report.
options.D: Renegotiate the firm's third-party logistics contracts to improve fleet-utilisation efficiency.

vignette: Olivia Andersen, People Director at an Oslo fintech with 180 employees, identifies that the firm's standard contract specifies a 'preferred availability' clause that disproportionately disadvantages caregivers (predominantly women), and that exit data show 14 women leaving in the past 18 months citing schedule rigidity.

options.A: Maintain the existing contract template but offer flexible-working as a discretionary line-manager benefit on a case-by-case basis.
options.B: Remove the 'preferred availability' clause from standard contracts, introduce a documented right to request flexible-working arrangements aligned with the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158, and offer existing staff the option to amend their contracts within 90 days.
options.C: Launch a 'work-life balance week' featuring wellness sessions and lunchtime talks.
options.D: Subscribe the firm to a corporate gym membership programme for all employees.

vignette: Marta Sánchez, Head of Corporate Governance at a Barcelona-listed industrials firm, has reviewed the board composition ahead of the 2026 deadline of the Women on Boards Directive. Of nine non-executive directors, two are women — a 22% representation that falls short of the 40% directive target.

options.A: Add a paragraph on diversity ambitions to the board's annual letter to shareholders.
options.B: Wait for natural board-seat turnover and only act if a vacancy occurs before the directive deadline.
options.C: Build a 24-month board-renewal plan that brings non-executive director composition to at least 40% of the under-represented sex by the end of June 2026, in line with Directive (EU) 2022/2381, with annual disclosure of progress in the corporate governance report.
options.D: Launch a women-in-engineering recruitment campaign at three regional universities.

vignette: Klaus Berger, Head of Leadership Development at a Frankfurt-based automotive supplier with 4,800 employees, reviews the senior-management pipeline and finds that women constitute 38% of mid-management but only 11% of director-level appointments over the past three years.

options.A: Run a high-visibility 'women in leadership' speaker series with quarterly executive-sponsored panels.
options.B: Establish a documented director-appointment pipeline-tracking process with gender-balanced shortlists, annual progression-rate audits, and a public commitment to 40% representation in management positions by 2027, mirroring the Commission's 50%-by-2024 internal target as an operational benchmark.
options.C: Continue the current succession process and document the gap in the people-strategy review for the next strategic cycle.
options.D: Implement a 360-degree feedback tool for all mid-managers regardless of gender.

vignette: Andrea Rossi, Chief of Staff at a Milan-listed financial services firm, has been asked to prepare the firm's response to a shareholder resolution on board diversity. The board is currently 18% female; the firm has no documented director-recruitment process and relies on personal-network referrals.

options.A: Replace the informal director-recruitment process with a documented one requiring gender-balanced shortlists and competence-based selection criteria, in alignment with Articles 5–6 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 on improving the gender balance among directors of listed companies.
options.B: Establish an unconscious-bias training programme for the board.
options.C: Engage the existing director-search firm with verbal instructions to broaden the candidate pool, without changing the documented process.
options.D: Prepare a detailed shareholder-meeting presentation showcasing the firm's broader diversity initiatives.

vignette: Helga Larsen, Nominations Committee Chair at an Oslo-listed energy firm, observes that the existing director-search process explicitly weights 'prior board experience,' a criterion that systematically disadvantages women in a sector where female board representation is low to start with.

options.A: Replace the prior-board-experience weighting with a competence-and-skills matrix derived from the firm's strategic priorities, and require gender-balanced shortlists for every director vacancy, consistent with Articles 4–5 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381.
options.B: Increase the number of board meetings per year to allow more substantive director engagement.
options.C: Issue a public statement that the firm welcomes board candidates from non-traditional backgrounds.
options.D: Retain the experience criterion but add a parallel 'high-potential' route that the committee can use selectively.

vignette: Mateusz Kowalczyk, Head of Talent Development at a Polish retail chain with 8,000 employees, finds that promotion decisions to regional-director level are taken in informal manager-only meetings with no documented criteria, and that women are nominated less than 25% of the time despite making up 45% of store-manager candidates.

options.A: Replace the informal nomination process with a documented promotion procedure including written criteria, gender-balanced nomination requirements, and an annual audit of promotion rates by gender to the regional-director level.
options.B: Roll out a customer-experience training initiative across all stores.
options.C: Launch a 'future leaders' branding campaign featuring high-performing female store managers.
options.D: Allow managers to continue the existing process but introduce an annual report on promotion outcomes for review by HR.

vignette: Ileana Marinescu, Head of Public Affairs at a Bucharest media group, is preparing the firm's voluntary disclosure on senior-management gender balance ahead of the next CSRD reporting cycle. The senior-management roster is 79% male; the executive committee is 100% male.

options.A: Schedule a series of public speaking engagements for the firm's small number of senior women to raise their visibility.
options.B: Set a documented internal target of 40% representation of the under-represented sex in senior-management roles by 2027, with annual progress disclosure in the firm's CSRD report and a transparent succession-planning process for the executive committee.
options.C: Continue current practice and report the existing gender breakdown in the CSRD disclosure without a forward target.
options.D: Commission an independent review of the firm's editorial-coverage gender balance.

vignette: Niamh O'Sullivan, Company Secretary at a Dublin-listed pharmaceutical firm, finds that the firm has not yet aligned its remuneration-committee composition with the gender-balance requirements of Directive (EU) 2022/2381, with all three remuneration-committee members being male despite the directive's 2026 deadline.

options.A: Schedule the reconstitution for the AGM following the next director-term expiry, two years out.
options.B: Issue a chair statement at the next AGM acknowledging the importance of board diversity for shareholder value.
options.C: Increase the number of independent directors on the audit committee.
options.D: Reconstitute the remuneration committee to ensure at least 40% representation of the under-represented sex, in line with Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381, ahead of the firm's next AGM.

vignette: Pieter de Jong, Head of HR at an Amsterdam-listed consumer-goods firm, has been asked to recommend whether the firm should adopt the alternative reporting regime under Directive (EU) 2022/2381 (focused on procedural changes) or the substantive 40% target. The current board has 30% female representation.

options.A: Adopt the alternative procedural-only reporting regime to preserve flexibility on the timing of board renewal.
options.B: Publish a corporate diversity declaration ahead of the next AGM.
options.C: Adopt the substantive 40% target under Directive (EU) 2022/2381 and document a board-renewal plan to reach the target by 30 June 2026, with annual progress reporting in the corporate governance statement.
options.D: Add a third independent non-executive director to strengthen audit oversight.

vignette: Yannis Papadopoulos, HR Director at an Athens-listed shipping group with a 13-member board, identifies that the firm's bonus structure for executive directors does not currently link any portion of variable pay to gender-balance progression at senior management levels, despite the firm's stated diversity ambition.

options.A: Issue a corporate values statement linking the firm's strategic priorities to its diversity commitment.
options.B: Defer the KPI introduction to the next remuneration-policy review, scheduled three years out under the current shareholder approval cycle.
options.C: Tie executive variable pay more strongly to share-price performance metrics.
options.D: Introduce a documented gender-balance KPI as a 10% weighting in executive-director variable pay, tied to measurable senior-management gender-representation milestones aligned with the Women on Boards Directive's spirit of accountability.

vignette: Camille Dubois, Group HR Lead at a Paris-headquartered listed retailer, reviews succession-planning data and finds that of 15 senior-leader retirements expected in the next five years, only two have a documented female successor identified, despite a balanced talent-pool composition at director-1 level.

options.A: Add a recommendation to the talent-review template suggesting line managers consider diverse successors.
options.B: Mandate gender-balanced succession panels for every senior role, document at least one qualified candidate of the under-represented sex per role, and integrate succession-pipeline gender metrics into the annual people-strategy review reported to the board.
options.C: Host an annual 'women in retail leadership' panel as part of the firm's diversity month.
options.D: Launch a retail-management graduate scheme targeting top-tier business-school graduates.

vignette: Antti Nieminen, Head of Governance at a Helsinki-listed paper manufacturer, observes that committee chair roles on the firm's board (audit, remuneration, nominations) are all currently held by men, despite the board itself being 36% female.

options.A: Rotate at least one committee chair role to a director of the under-represented sex at the next committee-renewal cycle, ensuring committee leadership composition aligns with the gender-balance objectives of Directive (EU) 2022/2381.
options.B: Add a new ESG committee with broad cross-functional remit.
options.C: Keep the current committee composition stable for continuity through the firm's strategic-plan execution period.
options.D: Highlight the firm's overall board-diversity progress in the next investor-relations roadshow.

vignette: Iulia Stancu, HR Director at a Bucharest-listed bank, finds that the bank's branch-manager appointments over the past five years have gone to men 71% of the time, despite an internal candidate pool that is 52% female, with no documented record of selection rationale.

options.A: Add a quarterly diversity-metrics dashboard to the senior-management review without changing the appointment process itself.
options.B: Migrate the bank's branch-management software to a unified national platform.
options.C: Run a 'branch leaders of tomorrow' internal communications campaign featuring high-potential female candidates.
options.D: Introduce a documented branch-manager-appointment process requiring gender-balanced shortlists and structured interview rubrics, audit the past three years' appointments for unexplained variance, and remediate identified gaps through targeted promotion in the next cycle.

vignette: Paolo Conti, Head of Talent at a Rome-listed utility, identifies that the firm's executive-development programme — a key feeder for senior leadership — has admitted only 18% women over the past four cohorts, despite gender-balanced applications. The selection panel is exclusively male.

options.A: Launch a 'women in executive development' alumni network with quarterly events.
options.B: Maintain the existing selection process but invite an external observer to attend the panel meetings.
options.C: Increase the duration of the executive-development programme from six to nine months.
options.D: Reconstitute the selection panel to ensure gender-balanced membership, introduce a documented selection rubric tied to leadership competencies, and target gender-balanced cohorts of the executive-development programme aligned with the Commission's 50%-by-2024 internal benchmark for management-level gender balance.

vignette: Eva Ström, Head of Investor Relations at a Stockholm-listed engineering firm, prepares the firm's response to an institutional investor query on the firm's compliance trajectory under the Women on Boards Directive. The firm currently has 33% female representation on its 15-member board.

options.A: Increase the number of independent non-executive directors to strengthen governance oversight.
options.B: Send a tailored investor letter highlighting the board's overall ESG credentials.
options.C: Acknowledge the directive's deadline in the response and indicate the firm will revisit composition targets as part of its next governance review.
options.D: Disclose a documented board-renewal plan that brings non-executive director composition to at least 40% of the under-represented sex by 30 June 2026, in line with Directive (EU) 2022/2381, alongside the procedural commitments under Article 5.

vignette: Kasper Madsen, Head of People at a Copenhagen-headquartered shipping firm, finds that the firm's senior-leadership-team meeting agendas are set exclusively by the CEO's chief of staff, and that female senior leaders raise agenda items 60% less frequently than male peers — a structural pattern unaddressed by current governance.

options.A: Migrate the firm's meeting-management software to a vendor with stronger collaboration features.
options.B: Maintain the current agenda-setting practice and trust the CEO's chief of staff to invite balanced contribution informally.
options.C: Establish a documented agenda-setting protocol allowing all senior-leadership-team members equal access to agenda inclusion, and audit agenda-item submission patterns by gender quarterly to identify and address structural inequities in voice.
options.D: Run a workshop on inclusive meeting practices for the senior-leadership team.

vignette: Jolanta Adamczyk, HR Manager at a Warsaw-listed insurance firm, identifies that the firm's leadership-pipeline disclosure in its CSRD report shows female representation at C-1 level dropping from 42% in 2023 to 31% in 2025, with no documented analysis of the regression.

options.A: Commission an internal root-cause analysis of the C-1 gender-representation regression, publish findings and a remediation plan in the next CSRD report, and re-establish progression-tracking governance aligned with the firm's stated gender-balance ambitions.
options.B: Launch a customer-satisfaction improvement programme across the firm's claims-handling teams.
options.C: Schedule a series of communications campaigns highlighting the firm's continuing commitment to leadership diversity.
options.D: Note the trend in the next CSRD report and commit to monitoring it during the next strategic-plan period.

vignette: Gergő Tóth, Head of Public Sector Practice at a Budapest-listed consulting firm, observes that the firm's bid-team leadership composition for major government tenders is structurally male — out of the past 22 lead-bid roles, 21 were filled by men, despite a balanced senior-management gender composition.

options.A: Maintain the current bid-staffing approach but ask team leads to consider gender balance informally when staffing future bids.
options.B: Adopt a new bid-pricing methodology to improve win rates in the public sector.
options.C: Issue a documented bid-team-staffing protocol requiring gender-balanced senior-team composition for major bids, monitor compliance through a quarterly review by the firm's executive committee, and publish gender-balance outcomes in the firm's annual people-strategy report.
options.D: Issue a press release celebrating recent senior female appointments at partner level.

vignette: Sigrid Aas, HR Director at an Oslo professional-services firm with 600 employees, learns that female partners are 24% of the partnership but receive only 12% of public-speaking invitations the firm allocates each year — a visibility gap that affects future-partner trajectory.

options.A: Establish a documented public-speaking-allocation process tied to expertise areas, ring-fence at least 40% of speaking slots for partners of the under-represented sex, and audit allocation outcomes annually as part of the partnership-governance review.
options.B: Continue the current allocation but encourage partners to nominate female colleagues for upcoming speaking events.
options.C: Feature female partners prominently in the firm's annual brochure.
options.D: Subscribe the firm to a media-training service available to all partners on a self-service basis.

vignette: Tomáš Novák, Head of Corporate Strategy at a Prague-listed manufacturer, finds that the firm's strategic-planning workshops — which materially shape resource allocation — are systematically attended by 90% male senior leaders, despite a 30% female senior-leadership composition, due to invitation patterns set by the COO.

options.A: Engage an external strategy consultancy to refresh the firm's three-year planning framework.
options.B: Reform the strategic-planning workshop invitation process to ensure all senior leaders of equivalent seniority are invited regardless of gender, and audit attendance and contribution patterns by gender as a standing item in the executive-committee review.
options.C: Maintain current invitation patterns but circulate workshop output more broadly across the senior-leadership team for input.
options.D: Add a 'diversity of perspective' principle to the firm's strategic-planning charter.

vignette: Lara Vukmir, Nominations Committee Secretary at a Zagreb-listed energy company, observes that the firm has not refreshed its director-skills matrix in five years, and that the matrix as currently written includes criteria that systematically reflect the existing board's profile, with no documented anchor in the firm's forward-strategic priorities.

options.A: Refresh the director-skills matrix in line with the firm's three-year strategic priorities, ensure criteria are gender-neutral and forward-looking, and require gender-balanced shortlists for the next director appointment, in alignment with Articles 4–5 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381.
options.B: Retain the current matrix but conduct an informal review with the existing search firm before the next vacancy.
options.C: Increase the number of board meetings per year to provide more time for directors to engage with strategic matters.
options.D: Publicise the existing matrix on the firm's investor-relations website to demonstrate transparency.

vignette: Andrei Popescu, Director of Legal & Compliance at a Bucharest-headquartered logistics firm with 1,800 employees across three sites, has received an internal report indicating six allegations of workplace sexual harassment in the past 12 months. The current grievance procedure references only 'inappropriate behaviour' and provides no specific channel for harassment cases.

options.A: Adopt a documented anti-harassment policy aligned with ILO Convention No. 190 on Violence and Harassment in the World of Work, including a confidential reporting channel, named investigation owners, victim-support provisions, and mandatory annual training for all line managers.
options.B: Commission an external review of the firm's broader employee-engagement metrics.
options.C: Update the existing grievance procedure with a single new paragraph mentioning harassment and continue handling cases under the existing process.
options.D: Issue a CEO email reaffirming the firm's zero-tolerance position on harassment.

vignette: Brigitte Müller, Head of People at a Munich-based automotive engineering firm with 600 employees, learns that a male manager has been the subject of three separate informal complaints from female direct reports in 18 months, alleging sexually inappropriate comments. No formal investigation has been opened. HR has handled each complaint as a 'workplace conflict.'

options.A: Quietly reassign the manager to a non-supervisory role to avoid the disruption of a formal investigation.
options.B: Send the manager and complainants to a joint mediation session led by an external workplace-conflict specialist.
options.C: Roll out a generic civility-at-work training programme for all employees in the affected division.
options.D: Open a formal investigation in line with the firm's harassment policy, suspend the manager's direct-line authority pending investigation outcome, and offer trauma-informed support to the complainants in alignment with the Victims' Rights Directive 2012/29/EU principles.

vignette: Anna Sokolova, Communications Director at a Riga-based media company, finds that two female journalists have received targeted online harassment campaigns following politically sensitive articles, including doxxing and threats of sexual violence. The firm has no documented protocol for online violence affecting employees.

options.A: Issue a public editorial denouncing the harassment of journalists and call for stronger platform regulation.
options.B: Subscribe the firm's social-media accounts to a brand-protection monitoring service.
options.C: Suggest the affected journalists temporarily reduce their byline visibility on contentious topics until the campaigns subside.
options.D: Adopt a documented protocol for online-harassment incidents involving employees, including legal-support coverage, platform-takedown coordination, and a counselling provision, aligned with the cyberviolence provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 on combating violence against women and domestic violence.

vignette: Pierre Lambert, Workplace Safety Manager at a Lyon-based industrial-services firm with 320 employees, identifies that the firm's late-shift operations expose women to safety risks: lone working, poorly lit access routes, and no documented protocol for incidents of harassment or assault during transit between site and parking.

options.A: Introduce a documented late-shift safety protocol covering lighting, paired-working arrangements, and an incident-reporting channel, aligned with the workplace-safety provisions of ILO Convention No. 190 and the firm's duty of care under EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC.
options.B: Encourage employees to use a shared rideshare app on late shifts and absorb the cost into existing transport budgets.
options.C: Upgrade the on-site CCTV system to a higher-resolution model.
options.D: Add a paragraph on late-shift safety to the next 'workplace wellness' newsletter.

vignette: Ester Vasilescu, Inclusion Lead at a Cluj-Napoca-based software firm with 280 employees, observes that the firm's recruitment marketing materials systematically depict men in 'technical' roles and women in 'support' roles, reinforcing gender stereotypes that the EU Strategy 2020-2025 explicitly identifies as a barrier to women in STEM.

options.A: Run a one-off social-media campaign for International Women's Day featuring senior female engineers.
options.B: Replace only the most prominent images on the recruitment landing page and revisit other materials in the next brand refresh cycle.
options.C: Audit and rebuild the firm's recruitment marketing materials to reflect gender-balanced representation across role types, and adopt a documented review checklist for new content, aligned with the Strategy's commitment to challenging stereotypes in education and the workplace.
options.D: Migrate the recruitment website to a faster-loading content platform.

vignette: Niko Mäkinen, Operations Director at a Helsinki-based hotel chain with 950 employees across 18 properties, learns that housekeeping staff (overwhelmingly women) are exposed to guest harassment in lone-cleaning situations, and that the firm's existing grievance process requires reporting to the property manager — frequently the perpetrator's drinking companion.

options.A: Add a 'safe stay' badge to the firm's website committing to respectful guest behaviour.
options.B: Renegotiate the firm's housekeeping-supplies contract to reduce per-room cost.
options.C: Introduce a documented guest-harassment protocol with a centralised reporting channel bypassing the property manager, victim-support provisions, and the right to refuse service to flagged guests, in alignment with the workplace-safety provisions of ILO Convention No. 190 against third-party violence.
options.D: Issue a verbal instruction to property managers to escalate harassment reports promptly.

vignette: Alžběta Dvořáková, HR Manager at a Prague-based call centre with 700 employees, identifies that 60% of customer-facing staff are women and that the firm has no documented protocol for handling abusive callers — a daily occurrence including sexually explicit verbal harassment. The current practice is for staff to 'stay polite and try to de-escalate.'

options.A: Run a 'how to handle difficult customers' workshop for new joiners during induction week.
options.B: Adopt a documented abusive-caller protocol granting staff an explicit right to terminate calls involving sexual or violent content, with mandatory incident logging and post-call support, aligned with ILO Convention No. 190's prohibition of third-party violence and harassment in the world of work.
options.C: Upgrade the call-centre's customer-relationship-management software to capture interaction data more precisely.
options.D: Update the existing call-handling script with a softer escalation path while maintaining customer-retention as the primary KPI.

vignette: Fionn O'Connor, Compliance Officer at a Dublin-based event-management firm with 140 employees and a 600-strong contractor pool, finds that contractor staff at large events have no clear point of contact for reporting harassment, and that of 22 incidents reported informally last year, none were investigated formally.

options.A: Extend the firm's harassment-reporting infrastructure to cover contractor staff, designate trained on-site harassment-response leads at every event, and include the protocol in the firm's contractor-onboarding pack, aligned with ILO Convention No. 190's coverage of all parties in the world of work.
options.B: Add a generic email address to the contractor handbook for reporting concerns.
options.C: Switch the firm's contractor-payment system to a faster digital provider.
options.D: Distribute branded 'we listen' lanyards to all contractor staff at major events.

vignette: Stéphane Robert, HR Lead at a Brussels-based EU-affairs consultancy with 110 employees, identifies that the firm's social events are systematically alcohol-centric and that informal feedback channels reveal multiple incidents of inappropriate comments at off-site team gatherings, none formally reported.

options.A: Continue current event practices but designate a senior leader as informal point of contact for any post-event concerns.
options.B: Negotiate a new corporate-rate agreement with a preferred event-venue partner.
options.C: Issue a memo asking employees to 'stay professional' at firm events.
options.D: Adopt a documented social-events conduct policy extending the firm's harassment policy to all firm-sponsored events, ensure non-alcoholic options are equally featured, and brief event hosts on bystander-intervention obligations, aligned with ILO Convention No. 190's broad workplace definition.

vignette: Maria Antoniou, Head of Security at a Larnaca-based hospitality group with 1,200 employees, learns that two female employees have disclosed they are experiencing domestic violence at home. The firm currently has no documented support framework, and both employees fear job impact if they request schedule changes.

options.A: Roll out an employee-assistance programme covering general mental-health support.
options.B: Display posters with the national domestic-violence helpline number in employee break rooms.
options.C: Adopt a documented domestic-violence workplace-support policy including paid safe leave, scheduling and location flexibility, and confidentiality protections, aligned with the workplace-support provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 on combating violence against women and domestic violence.
options.D: Treat each employee request on a case-by-case discretionary basis to avoid setting a costly precedent.

vignette: Lukas Schwarz, Talent Acquisition Lead at a Vienna-based engineering firm with 460 employees, observes that the firm's interview-panel composition produces consistent under-evaluation of female candidates: of 15 final-stage interviews in the past year, all-male panels rejected female candidates 70% of the time, mixed panels at 45%.

options.A: Add a guidance note to the hiring playbook recommending panel diversity where 'practical.'
options.B: Launch a 'women in engineering' employer-brand campaign on LinkedIn.
options.C: Subscribe the firm to an applicant-tracking system with stronger candidate-pipeline analytics.
options.D: Mandate gender-mixed interview panels for all final-stage hires, introduce structured-interview rubrics with documented competence-based scoring, and audit panel-outcome variance by panel composition annually as part of the firm's hiring-equity review.

vignette: Gianna Rossi, Inclusion Manager at a Naples-based fashion retailer with 850 employees, identifies that the firm's product-marketing campaigns repeatedly use imagery sexualising young women in workplace settings — depictions that internal staff surveys flag as contributing to a 'lookist' workplace culture and to harassment incidents.

options.A: Retain the existing creative direction for the current season's campaign and revisit the policy at the next brand-strategy refresh.
options.B: Feature the firm's senior female executives in an upcoming 'leaders behind the brand' campaign.
options.C: Adopt a documented marketing-content review policy aligned with the EU Strategy's commitment to challenging gender stereotypes, including a checklist against sexualising imagery, and integrate the policy into creative-brief sign-off, in collaboration with the firm's CSR and HR functions.
options.D: Migrate the firm's media-buying to a programmatic-advertising platform to reduce campaign costs.

vignette: Marek Dvořák, Manufacturing Plant Manager at a Brno industrial site with 480 employees, learns that female apprentices report being given systematically lighter tasks than male peers — 'protective' assignments that reinforce stereotypes about women's physical capacity and limit their skill development.

options.A: Maintain the existing assignment practice and introduce 'enrichment opportunities' for high-performing female apprentices on a discretionary basis.
options.B: Upgrade the plant's safety-equipment procurement to a higher-specification supplier.
options.C: Replace the informal task-assignment practice with a documented competence-based rotation programme covering all apprentices regardless of gender, audit task-assignment patterns quarterly, and brief shift supervisors on the EU Strategy's commitment to challenging stereotypes in education and training.
options.D: Run a 'women in manufacturing' celebration day during apprenticeship-recruitment season.

vignette: Bjørn Eriksen, Crisis-Response Coordinator at an Oslo-based gas utility with 2,200 employees, finds that the firm's emergency-response protocol does not include a specific provision for victims of domestic violence whose safety depends on workplace location and schedule confidentiality.

options.A: Address such cases reactively when they occur, drawing on the firm's existing employee-assistance budget.
options.B: Distribute leaflets on workplace safety to all employees during awareness week.
options.C: Update the firm's general-emergency-response training to a new digital simulation platform.
options.D: Integrate a documented domestic-violence-response provision into the emergency-response protocol, including emergency-relocation support, schedule-change confidentiality, and trained HR contacts, aligned with Directive (EU) 2024/1385.

vignette: Inês Carvalho, Compliance Officer at a Porto-based hospitality group with 380 employees, identifies that female employees on overnight shifts have to walk through a poorly-lit external corridor to reach restrooms shared with the public — a layout that has produced two reported incidents of harassment in 18 months.

options.A: Reroute access to staff-only restrooms, install adequate lighting on existing access paths, and document the change in the firm's workplace-safety register, in line with the firm's duty of care under EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC and ILO Convention No. 190's third-party-violence provisions.
options.B: Issue a notice asking employees to use the buddy system on overnight shifts when accessing the affected facilities.
options.C: Refurbish the firm's main reception lobby to improve guest impressions.
options.D: Display 'staff safety matters' signage in the affected corridor.

vignette: Lina Andersson, Head of Compliance at a Gothenburg-based logistics firm with 590 employees, finds that the company's grievance procedure currently routes all harassment complaints through the line manager, but that 80% of complaints involve the line manager themselves or a peer-relationship.

options.A: Migrate the firm's HR-case-management software to a unified ticketing platform.
options.B: Add a footnote to the grievance procedure clarifying that employees may escalate to HR if uncomfortable with their line manager.
options.C: Replace the line-manager-only grievance route with a documented dual-channel procedure offering an alternative confidential reporting line outside the chain of command, in alignment with Article 10 of ILO Convention No. 190 on safe and effective dispute mechanisms.
options.D: Issue a CEO email reaffirming open-door access to senior management for any concerns.

vignette: Marc Vermeulen, HR Director at an Antwerp-based law firm with 250 employees including support staff, identifies that the firm's longstanding 'partner-track culture' includes informal late-night networking events at which inappropriate behaviour by senior partners has been historically tolerated, with two associates resigning in the past year citing this dynamic.

options.A: Issue a written reminder to the partnership about professional conduct expectations and continue current networking practices.
options.B: Refresh the firm's website branding to better appeal to younger associate candidates.
options.C: Adopt a documented partner-conduct standard extending the firm's harassment policy to all firm-sanctioned networking activities, conduct anonymous culture-survey diagnostics, and tie partner profit-share allocation to documented compliance, aligned with ILO Convention No. 190's broad workplace definition.
options.D: Convene a senior-partners' offsite focused on 'culture and values' renewal.

vignette: Eleni Vasileiou, Communications Lead at a Thessaloniki-based public-relations firm with 90 employees, observes that the firm's standard press-release templates default to gendered descriptors ('businessmen,' 'salesman') and that the marketing team has resisted updating them as 'too disruptive.'

options.A: Update all template copy to gender-neutral terminology in line with the EU Strategy's commitment to challenging stereotypes through language, brief the marketing team on the rationale, and document the change in the firm's editorial-style guide.
options.B: Issue a 'words matter' awareness-week campaign across the firm's social channels.
options.C: Procure a new content-management system to streamline press-release distribution.
options.D: Update the most-used templates only and leave secondary templates for the next style-guide refresh.

vignette: Alessandro Greco, Head of Operations at a Rome-based ride-share platform with 320 in-house staff and 4,500 driver-partners, learns that female passengers report harassment by drivers at a rate three times the platform's stated tolerance threshold, and that the platform's incident-handling process currently relies on app-based ratings without a dedicated harassment workflow.

options.A: Tighten the existing rating-based moderation rules and introduce a soft suspension threshold, while preserving driver-supply during peak hours.
options.B: Invest in upgrading the passenger app's user-interface design.
options.C: Introduce a documented harassment-response workflow for passenger reports, including immediate driver suspension pending investigation, victim-support liaison, and mandatory driver onboarding aligned with ILO Convention No. 190's third-party-violence provisions and the cyberviolence and platform-violence dimensions addressed in Directive (EU) 2024/1385.
options.D: Launch a passenger-safety campaign featuring tips for safer rides.

vignette: Anja Krüger, Head of HR at a Berlin-based pharmaceutical firm with 720 employees, identifies that the firm's exit-interview data reveals 11 departures in 18 months citing 'cultural fit' issues, all of them women, with multiple references to dismissive treatment in mixed-gender meetings — a pattern not surfaced through current grievance channels.

options.A: Run an internal 'inclusive meetings' workshop series for all line managers.
options.B: Adjust the exit-interview template to capture more granular feedback going forward, while continuing existing practices.
options.C: Commission a confidential structured culture-audit by an external specialist focused on gendered workplace dynamics, publish action commitments tied to findings, and integrate exit-interview gender-pattern analysis into quarterly people-strategy review, in alignment with the EU Strategy's commitment to addressing workplace culture as a stereotype barrier.
options.D: Migrate the firm's HR-information system to a vendor with stronger people-analytics capabilities.

vignette: Elena Marchetti, Programme Director at a Florence-based research institute applying for Horizon Europe funding, finds that the institute has no Gender Equality Plan in place, despite the eligibility requirement for public-research applicants since 2022. The institute's 14-page draft 'diversity statement' does not meet the GEP criteria.

options.A: Develop and publish a Gender Equality Plan meeting the five mandatory Horizon Europe criteria — public document, dedicated resources, data collection and monitoring, training, and gender-balance targets — before submitting the application, in alignment with the Horizon Europe gender-equality plan eligibility requirement.
options.B: Increase the budget allocated to the proposed research project's outreach activities.
options.C: Submit the application with the existing diversity statement and request a derogation from the GEP requirement based on the institute's small size.
options.D: Publish a one-page 'commitment to gender equality' statement on the institute's homepage ahead of the submission deadline.

vignette: Hanna Lehtinen, Sustainability Lead at an Espoo-based clean-energy firm with 410 employees, is preparing the firm's response to a public-tender requirement on the gender dimension of the proposed Green Deal-aligned project. The current proposal has no documented gender-impact assessment.

options.A: Partner with a local NGO active in the renewable-energy sector to broaden the project's community engagement.
options.B: Conduct a documented gender-impact assessment of the proposed project, identify how the energy-transition activities affect women, men, and non-binary stakeholders differentially, and integrate mitigations into the project plan, in line with the Strategy's commitment to gender mainstreaming across the green transition.
options.C: Add a 'gender-equality is central to our work' section to the proposal's introduction.
options.D: Insert a generic paragraph referencing the firm's overall ESG commitments and proceed with the current project plan.

vignette: Carlos Méndez, Diversity Officer at a Seville-based municipal authority with 2,800 employees, finds that the authority's procurement policies do not currently include any gender-impact criterion, despite procuring services in domains (childcare, eldercare, public transport) where gendered patterns of use are well documented.

options.A: Apply the new criterion to contracts above a high financial threshold only, to avoid administrative overhead on smaller procurements.
options.B: Modernise the authority's e-procurement platform with a more user-friendly supplier portal.
options.C: Introduce a documented gender-impact criterion into the authority's procurement framework, requiring suppliers in care, transport, and public-services contracts to demonstrate consideration of differential gender impact, in line with the Strategy's commitment to gender mainstreaming in public-policy delivery.
options.D: Add a 'gender-aware procurement' principle to the authority's published values.

vignette: Sara Kaur, Equality and Diversity Manager at a Birmingham-based NHS Trust with 6,500 staff, identifies that the trust's clinical-pathway audits do not currently disaggregate health-outcome data by gender intersected with ethnicity — despite published evidence that racialised women face systematically worse outcomes in maternal-care pathways.

options.A: Display posters celebrating the trust's diverse workforce in patient-facing areas.
options.B: Continue current audit practice but flag the issue for the next clinical-governance review cycle.
options.C: Roll out a patient-experience survey tool across all outpatient clinics.
options.D: Introduce intersectional disaggregation (gender × ethnicity × disability) in clinical-outcome audits for high-impact pathways, publish findings annually, and develop targeted interventions for identified disparities, aligned with the EU Cancer Plan and broader Strategy commitments to intersectional gender mainstreaming in health policy.

vignette: Janneke Bakker, Programme Manager at an Utrecht-based digital-skills foundation working with migrant and refugee women, finds that the foundation's standard skills-assessment tool was designed for native-language speakers and produces systematically lower scores for trainees with limited Dutch — disproportionately women, given the demographic profile.

options.A: Translate the foundation's marketing materials into the most common trainee languages.
options.B: Renegotiate the foundation's office-rental contract to reduce overhead.
options.C: Redesign the assessment tool with multilingual support and culturally sensitive design, validate the redesigned tool against a representative sample, and document the methodology in alignment with the Strategy's intersectional commitment addressing migrant-status disadvantage in addition to gender.
options.D: Add a manual moderation step where assessors can adjust scores for limited-language speakers on a case-by-case basis.

vignette: Robert Kovács, Strategic Planning Lead at a Budapest-based public-transport authority, observes that the network's route-planning model does not factor in gendered patterns of mobility (multi-stop 'trip-chaining,' off-peak care-related travel), with the consequence that off-peak service frequencies disadvantage women's mobility patterns.

options.A: Run a public-engagement campaign celebrating women's contributions to the city's mobility.
options.B: Procure a new electric-bus fleet to reduce the network's carbon footprint.
options.C: Maintain the current model but add a quarterly gender-breakdown report to the strategic-planning review.
options.D: Integrate gender-disaggregated mobility data into the network's route-planning model, target service-frequency upgrades for routes serving care-economy mobility patterns, and document the methodology change in the authority's strategic plan, in line with the Strategy's commitment to gender mainstreaming in transport policy.

vignette: Thierry Berger, Head of Innovation at a Strasbourg-based research consortium, finds that the consortium's medical-device development pipeline historically has tested products on male-dominated cohorts, with the consequence that several devices show reduced efficacy in female users — a known issue across the medical-technology sector.

options.A: Apply the new requirement to flagship clinical trials only, on resource-allocation grounds for smaller exploratory studies.
options.B: Add a 'sex and gender in research' principle to the consortium's published values.
options.C: Upgrade the consortium's research-data-management infrastructure to a unified cloud platform.
options.D: Mandate documented sex-and-gender-disaggregated analysis at every clinical-trial stage in the consortium's pipeline, in line with EMA guidance and the Horizon Europe requirement that gender be integrated into research content and methodology where relevant, and publish findings in the consortium's annual scientific report.

vignette: Anastasia Petrova, Inclusion Manager at a Sofia-based civil-society foundation that runs vocational-training programmes, identifies that the foundation's eligibility criteria implicitly exclude Roma women — for example, requiring a fixed home address that disproportionately disqualifies applicants from informal settlements.

options.A: Maintain the existing criteria but allow individual case-by-case waivers at the programme manager's discretion.
options.B: Feature Roma graduates in the foundation's annual-impact report.
options.C: Translate the foundation's website into a wider range of languages.
options.D: Revise the foundation's eligibility criteria to remove indirect discrimination against Roma women, integrate intersectional outreach pathways with Roma-community organisations, and document the change in alignment with the Strategy's intersectional commitment addressing the situation of Roma women specifically.

vignette: Tina Verhoeven, Director of Public Policy at a Brussels-based industry association, has been asked to coordinate the association's response to a Commission consultation on the Just Transition Mechanism. The current draft response makes no reference to differential gender impact of green-transition job displacement.

options.A: Schedule a high-profile sector roundtable on the Just Transition Mechanism for the autumn.
options.B: Submit the existing draft and address the gender dimension in a follow-up paper if requested by the Commission.
options.C: Commission a documented gender-impact analysis of the green-transition job-displacement scenarios in the association's sectoral footprint, integrate findings into the consultation response, and propose targeted reskilling pathways for women in carbon-intensive industries, in alignment with the Strategy's gender-mainstreaming approach to the Green Deal.
options.D: Add a single sentence to the consultation response affirming the association's commitment to a 'just and inclusive transition.'

vignette: Marek Tomaszewski, Programme Lead at a Gdańsk-based digital-inclusion charity, finds that the charity's standard intervention design has not been tested with older women (65+), the demographic with the largest digital-inclusion gap in the region, and that current materials assume mid-career professional contexts.

options.A: Redesign and pilot intervention materials specifically for older women, integrate age-and-gender intersectional considerations into all programme design templates, and document the methodology in alignment with the Strategy's intersectional commitment addressing age-related disadvantage in addition to gender.
options.B: Run a media campaign celebrating older women in the digital age.
options.C: Add a separate 'silver track' brochure highlighting which existing modules are 'most suitable' for older participants.
options.D: Subscribe the charity to a fundraising-management platform to reduce administrative overhead.

vignette: Aino Korhonen, Programme Director at a Tampere-based vocational-training agency, observes that the agency's standard placement-support service is poorly accessed by women with disabilities — only 4% of its clients in the past two years, against a 14% disability prevalence in the regional female labour pool.

options.A: Audit the agency's placement-support service for accessibility barriers, redesign the service in consultation with disability-led organisations and women's-employment organisations, and integrate the redesigned service into the agency's quality-management system, in line with the Strategy's intersectional commitment addressing disability and gender jointly.
options.B: Continue the existing service but commit to discussing the issue at the next quarterly board meeting.
options.C: Renegotiate the agency's office-cleaning contract to reduce overhead.
options.D: Update the agency's website to feature an 'accessibility' section listing disability-related contact details.

vignette: Pierre Dubois, Director of Research Programmes at a Lille-based academic research foundation, learns that the foundation's research-grant evaluation panels have not been required to consider the gender dimension of the proposed research, and that recent grant outcomes show the strongest performers on this dimension are systematically defunded against weaker proposals on traditional metrics.

options.A: Add the criterion as a non-scoring 'desirable feature' rather than a scoring component, to preserve continuity of evaluation outcomes.
options.B: Migrate the foundation's grant-management software to a more efficient cloud platform.
options.C: Add a 'gender in research' page to the foundation's website.
options.D: Introduce a documented gender-dimension evaluation criterion into the foundation's research-grant scoring framework, train grant-evaluation panels on its application, and disclose the change in the foundation's annual research-strategy report, in alignment with the Horizon Europe approach of integrating gender into research content where relevant.

vignette: Sandra Williams, Equalities Lead at a Manchester-based metropolitan-council administration with 4,200 employees, finds that the council's policy-development templates do not currently include a gender-equality impact-assessment field, despite evidence from peer councils that such assessments routinely surface unintended differential impacts.

options.A: Issue a council leader statement reaffirming commitment to equality across all council functions.
options.B: Introduce a mandatory gender-equality impact-assessment field on every policy-development template, train policy authors on its use, and integrate completed assessments into the council's published policy-decision documentation, in alignment with the Strategy's commitment to gender mainstreaming in public-administration practice.
options.C: Pilot the impact-assessment requirement on two policy areas (housing, transport) only, and revisit broader rollout in the next strategic-planning cycle.
options.D: Replace the council's intranet-content-management platform with a more modern alternative.

vignette: Magnus Olsson, Director of Strategic Planning at a Stockholm-based regional health agency, observes that the agency's mental-health service-design protocols do not currently address the gendered dimensions of mental-health presentation, despite well-documented evidence that women's symptoms are systematically under-recognised in current diagnostic pathways.

options.A: Procure a new electronic-health-record system to streamline clinical workflows.
options.B: Update the agency's mental-health service-design protocols with documented gender-sensitive diagnostic criteria, train clinical staff on gendered symptom presentation, and integrate gender-disaggregated outcome data into the agency's quality-improvement cycle, in line with the Strategy's gender-mainstreaming approach to health policy.
options.C: Add a recommendation to clinicians to 'consider gender' when applying existing diagnostic criteria, without protocol revision.
options.D: Launch a public-awareness campaign on women's mental health during the agency's annual health week.

vignette: Joanna Nowicka, Innovation Manager at a Wrocław-based AI ethics consortium, finds that the consortium's algorithmic-impact-assessment framework — used by member firms in their AI-procurement processes — does not currently include intersectional fairness testing (gender × ethnicity × age), despite extensive academic evidence on intersectional algorithmic harm.

options.A: Migrate the consortium's website to a more modern content-management system.
options.B: Update the algorithmic-impact-assessment framework with documented intersectional fairness testing requirements, publish the methodology, and brief member firms on adoption, in alignment with the EU Strategy's commitment to addressing AI bias as a stereotype-perpetuating technology requiring intersectional gender-mainstreaming consideration.
options.C: Add a footnote to the framework recommending intersectional testing where 'feasible' for member firms.
options.D: Host a high-profile conference on 'AI ethics and equality' featuring senior speakers from member firms.

vignette: Gianni Romano, Programme Director at a Naples-based labour-market reintegration agency, learns that the agency's standard employer-engagement protocols do not currently surface the specific employment barriers facing migrant women, who comprise 28% of the agency's caseload but only 9% of placements.

options.A: Renegotiate the agency's IT-services contract to improve cost efficiency.
options.B: Develop a documented intersectional employer-engagement protocol that includes targeted outreach to inclusive employers, language-and-credentials-recognition support, and specific placement-tracking for migrant women, in alignment with the Strategy's intersectional commitment to migrant women's employment.
options.C: Run a media campaign featuring successful placement stories of migrant women.
options.D: Continue the existing protocols and refer migrant women clients to a partner NGO for additional support.

vignette: Eliška Černá, Sustainability Director at a Prague-based industrial conglomerate, finds that the firm's CSRD-aligned materiality assessment treats gender equality as a single workforce metric, missing the differential impact of supply-chain practices on women in upstream geographies — a significant omission given the firm's textile sourcing.

options.A: Renegotiate the firm's logistics contracts to reduce supply-chain carbon emissions.
options.B: Expand the materiality assessment to include supply-chain gender-equality impacts, integrate findings into supplier engagement protocols, and disclose results under the CSRD's value-chain reporting requirements, in alignment with the Strategy's intersectional and gender-mainstreaming approach extended through the value chain.
options.C: Maintain the current scope of the materiality assessment for this reporting cycle and revisit value-chain extension at the next CSRD review.
options.D: Add a paragraph on the firm's commitment to women's empowerment to the sustainability-report introduction.

vignette: Marie Lefèvre, Programme Lead at a Paris-based smart-city public-private partnership, observes that the partnership's urban-planning models do not currently integrate gender-disaggregated public-space-use data, despite extensive published evidence that gender shapes patterns of public-space use, perception of safety, and accessibility needs.

options.A: Maintain current planning models but commit to a gender-perspective workshop in the next annual planning retreat.
options.B: Run a 'women's voices in city planning' communications campaign.
options.C: Procure a new GIS platform to improve overall urban-planning data integration.
options.D: Integrate gender-disaggregated public-space-use data into the partnership's urban-planning models, conduct gender-and-safety audits of priority urban areas, and document the methodology in the partnership's annual planning report, in line with the Strategy's gender-mainstreaming approach to urban policy.

vignette: Wim Janssen, Strategic Planning Lead at a Rotterdam-based port authority, finds that the port's workforce-development plan — covering 6,000 directly-employed and contractor workers — has not been disaggregated by gender or by intersectional categories, despite well-documented disparities in the maritime sector.

options.A: Disaggregate the workforce-development plan by gender and key intersectional categories (migrant status, age), introduce targeted recruitment and retention measures based on identified gaps, and integrate disaggregated reporting into the authority's annual workforce-strategy disclosure, aligned with the Strategy's mainstreaming commitments.
options.B: Apply the disaggregation requirement to the next planning cycle's strategic-roles only, due to data-collection constraints.
options.C: Invest in upgrading the port's container-handling automation infrastructure.
options.D: Showcase the port's existing women-in-maritime employees in the next sustainability report.

vignette: Adriana Costa, Regional Development Officer at a Coimbra-based EU-funds management authority, identifies that the authority's project-evaluation guidance does not require beneficiaries to demonstrate consideration of gender mainstreaming in EU-funded projects above €500,000, despite the funding's regulatory framework requiring it.

options.A: Pilot the new requirement on a subset of project types in the next funding round and assess feasibility before broader application.
options.B: Update the project-evaluation guidance to require documented gender-mainstreaming evidence for all EU-funded projects above the regulatory threshold, in alignment with the Common Provisions Regulation gender-mainstreaming requirements and the Strategy's broader gender-mainstreaming framework, and integrate compliance into the authority's monitoring cycle.
options.C: Migrate the authority's beneficiary-management software to a more modern cloud-based platform.
options.D: Run a training day on 'gender equality in EU-funded projects' for beneficiary organisations.

vignette: Claudia Hernández, Director of External Programmes at a Madrid-based development NGO administering EU-funded projects in West Africa, finds that the NGO's project-design templates for the next GAP III-aligned funding cycle do not include the mandatory minimum 85% gender-equality marker (G1 or G2) required by the Commission's external-action programming.

options.A: Update all project-design templates to ensure that 85% of new external-action programmes carry a G1 or G2 gender-equality marker, in alignment with the Gender Action Plan III (2021-2025) targets, and integrate marker-tracking into the NGO's monitoring-and-evaluation framework.
options.B: Renew the NGO's office-rental agreement at improved commercial terms.
options.C: Apply the 85% target to flagship projects only and treat smaller projects as exempt for resource-management reasons.
options.D: Highlight the NGO's existing women-focused programmes in the next annual report.

vignette: Luca Marchetti, Senior Programme Officer at a Brussels-based EU-funded democracy programme, learns that the programme's election-observation methodology does not currently disaggregate observed practices by gender — for example, voter-registration barriers, female-candidate ballot positioning, women's access to polling stations.

options.A: Add a 'women in democracy' panel to the programme's annual conference agenda.
options.B: Integrate documented gender-disaggregated indicators into the election-observation methodology, train observation teams on gender-sensitive observation, and report findings in line with the Gender Action Plan III's commitment to women's political participation in third countries.
options.C: Upgrade the programme's mission-management software to a more efficient platform.
options.D: Train one observer per mission as a 'gender focal point' and add a brief gender section to the existing reporting template.

vignette: Sophie Beaumont, EU Liaison Officer at a Lyon-based research consortium administering Horizon Europe funds for international cooperation, finds that the consortium's Research and Innovation Action proposals systematically allocate less than 30% of project funding to female lead researchers, despite a balanced researcher-pool composition.

options.A: Migrate the consortium's grant-management software to a unified system.
options.B: Apply the new policy from the next funding cycle and grandfather current-cycle allocations.
options.C: Highlight the female lead researchers' contributions in the consortium's annual report.
options.D: Adopt a documented funding-allocation policy ensuring at least 40% of Horizon Europe project leadership is held by researchers of the under-represented sex, in line with the Horizon Europe gender-equality commitments and the EU Strategy's mainstreaming approach to research funding.

vignette: Ashraf Ahmed, Programme Manager at a Berlin-based development bank running EU-financed credit-line programmes for SMEs in Eastern Partnership countries, identifies that the bank's borrower-screening criteria do not currently track the gender of the SME owner or the gender composition of the firm's workforce.

options.A: Introduce documented gender-disaggregated borrower tracking, integrate gender-balance criteria into the bank's loan-eligibility scoring with a target of 40% loans to women-owned SMEs by 2027, and align the framework with the Gender Action Plan III's commitment to women's economic empowerment in partner countries.
