options.B: Add an optional self-declaration field on loan applications and analyse the resulting data at the end of the financing cycle.
options.C: Negotiate revised partnership terms with regional commercial-bank partners to improve cost-efficiency.
options.D: Run an annual 'women entrepreneurs of the year' awards programme.

vignette: Eva Strömqvist, Trade Policy Adviser at a Stockholm-based Ministry of Foreign Affairs delegation to bilateral trade negotiations, observes that the Ministry's draft sustainability impact assessment for the trade agreement does not currently include a documented gender-impact analysis, despite Commission guidance to integrate gender into trade and Sustainable Development chapters.

options.A: Schedule additional bilateral meetings on agricultural-products tariff lines.
options.B: Conduct a documented gender-impact assessment of the proposed trade agreement, identify differential impacts on women's employment, entrepreneurship, and consumer prices in partner countries, and propose mitigations through the trade-and-sustainable-development chapter, in alignment with the Strategy's commitment to integrating gender into the EU's external trade policy.
options.C: Add a 'commitment to gender equality' paragraph to the agreement's preamble.
options.D: Include a brief reference to gender equality in the sustainable-development chapter and conduct a fuller analysis post-agreement.

vignette: François Mercier, Project Officer at a Brussels-based European Investment Bank-administered facility funding climate-resilience projects in Sub-Saharan Africa, finds that the facility's monitoring framework does not currently disaggregate beneficiary data by gender, missing differential impacts of climate financing on women in agriculture-dependent communities.

options.A: Introduce documented gender-disaggregated beneficiary tracking, integrate intersectional indicators (gender × livelihood × disability) into the monitoring framework, and report findings in line with GAP III's commitment to gender-responsive climate-action programming and the Strategy's mainstreaming approach.
options.B: Feature women beneficiaries prominently in the facility's annual impact-storytelling materials.
options.C: Renegotiate the facility's project-implementation-partner contracts to improve cost efficiency.
options.D: Pilot gender-disaggregation on a subset of high-priority projects before considering full rollout.

vignette: Eleni Kontari, Cooperation Officer at an Athens-based EU-funded regional programme working with Mediterranean partner countries, identifies that the programme's capacity-building offerings have low female participation rates (under 20%) — a structural pattern that has not been analysed or addressed despite three years of data.

options.A: Run a 'women in cooperation' showcase event highlighting existing female alumni.
options.B: Migrate the programme's online-learning platform to a more efficient vendor.
options.C: Commission a documented analysis of the structural barriers to female participation, redesign the capacity-building offering with female-only and mixed cohorts, and target gender-balanced participation rates by 2027, in alignment with the Gender Action Plan III's commitment to women's leadership in partner countries.
options.D: Add a recommendation to programme partners to nominate female participants where feasible.

vignette: Mateusz Kowalski, ESF+ Coordinator at a Warsaw-based regional managing authority, finds that the authority's project-selection criteria for the next European Social Fund Plus round do not include gender-mainstreaming as a scored evaluation criterion, despite the regulatory requirement.

options.A: Add a 'gender equality is a priority' page to the authority's call-for-proposals website.
options.B: Modernise the authority's project-management software to streamline beneficiary reporting.
options.C: Treat gender-mainstreaming as a non-scoring 'selection-stage check' rather than a competitive criterion.
options.D: Integrate gender-mainstreaming as a scored evaluation criterion in the project-selection methodology, train evaluation panels on its application, and disclose criterion weightings in the call documentation, in alignment with the European Social Fund Plus Regulation and the Strategy's mainstreaming framework.

vignette: Anita Verma, Senior Adviser at a Copenhagen-based development cooperation agency, observes that the agency's bilateral partnership agreements with three priority countries do not currently include explicit gender-equality benchmarks tied to disbursement, despite GAP III guidance on integrating gender into financing modalities.

options.A: Note the GAP III guidance in the next agency strategy review and revisit incorporation in the post-2026 framework.
options.B: Renegotiate the agency's translation-services contract to reduce administrative costs.
options.C: Add a 'gender equality is at the heart of our partnerships' clause to all renewed agreements.
options.D: Negotiate the inclusion of documented gender-equality benchmarks (e.g., women's share of programme leadership, gender-disaggregated outcome targets) tied to disbursement in renewed partnership agreements, in alignment with GAP III's commitment to result-based gender-equality commitments in bilateral cooperation.

vignette: Jorge Fonseca, Programme Director at a Lisbon-based EU-funded humanitarian agency working in South Sudan, finds that the agency's emergency-response protocols do not currently address the differential vulnerabilities of women and girls during humanitarian crises — including gender-based violence prevention and access to maternal-health services in conflict zones.

options.A: Procure a new fleet of emergency-response vehicles to improve operational logistics.
options.B: Adopt and integrate the IASC Gender with Age Marker into all humanitarian-response operations, target a minimum 80% of operations marked Code 3 or 4 (gender-sensitive or gender-responsive), and align with GAP III's commitment to gender-responsive humanitarian action and the Commission's humanitarian-aid gender-policy framework.
options.C: Issue a public statement on International Women's Day affirming the agency's commitment to women in crisis settings.
options.D: Add a 'consider gender' note to the existing emergency-response checklists and revisit fuller integration in the next strategic review.

vignette: Rosa Conti, Programme Officer at a Rome-based EU-funded technical-assistance facility supporting public-finance reforms in partner countries, identifies that the facility's diagnostic tools do not currently include gender-responsive budgeting analysis, despite Commission guidance on its centrality to GAP III.

options.A: Pilot the gender-responsive budgeting tool in two partner countries during the next cycle and assess feasibility before wider rollout.
options.B: Migrate the facility's project-tracking software to a more efficient cloud-based platform.
options.C: Integrate gender-responsive budgeting analysis into the facility's diagnostic toolkit, train country teams on its application, and report annually on partner-country uptake, in alignment with GAP III's commitment to integrating gender-responsive budgeting in public-financial-management reform support.
options.D: Run a high-profile workshop on 'gender and public finance' at the facility's annual conference.

vignette: Marko Janković, Head of Programmes at a Ljubljana-based EU-funded enlargement-policy support facility, learns that the facility's Western Balkans engagement portfolio does not currently include explicit programming on women's participation in political reform processes, despite the Commission's gender-equality acquis-alignment expectations.

options.A: Add a 'women and EU integration' panel to the next facility annual conference.
options.B: Develop documented programming targeting women's participation in political-reform processes in Western Balkans partner countries, integrate gender-disaggregated outcome indicators into the facility's results framework, and align programming with GAP III's commitment to women's political participation in pre-accession contexts.
options.C: Add a gender-equality dimension to existing programmes through small targeted top-ups, deferring stand-alone programming to the post-2026 cycle.
options.D: Renegotiate the facility's interpretation-services contract to reduce overhead costs.

vignette: Lena Müller, Trade and Development Officer at a Hamburg-based regional cooperation programme, observes that the programme's value-chain support to women coffee producers in East Africa lacks documented integration with broader market-access measures, with the consequence that supported women cooperatives are unable to translate production gains into revenue gains.

options.A: Continue current production-support activities and add a 'market-readiness' workshop in the final year of the programme.
options.B: Restructure the value-chain support to integrate market-access components — buyer-linkage, certification, logistics — alongside production support, with documented gender-disaggregated outcome indicators, in alignment with GAP III's commitment to women's economic empowerment through value-chain interventions.
options.C: Renegotiate the programme's transport contract with regional logistics partners.
options.D: Run a high-profile 'women in coffee' international showcase at the next sector trade fair.

vignette: Maria Ribeiro, Programme Lead at a Porto-based EU-funded migration-cooperation programme working with North African partners, finds that the programme's reintegration-support packages for returnees do not currently address the specific reintegration challenges facing women returnees, including stigma, economic-reintegration barriers, and gender-based violence vulnerability.

options.A: Develop a documented reintegration-support track tailored to the specific challenges facing women returnees, integrate gender-disaggregated outcome indicators into the programme's M&E framework, and align with GAP III's commitment to gender-responsive migration cooperation.
options.B: Feature successful women returnees in the programme's communications materials.
options.C: Add a one-day 'gender awareness' module to the existing reintegration training package.
options.D: Renegotiate the programme's IT-infrastructure contract to improve cost-efficiency.

vignette: Niamh Walsh, Senior Programme Officer at a Dublin-based EU-funded peace-and-conflict programme operating in fragile-state contexts, observes that women's representation on programme-supported peace-mediation tables remains below 15% — a structural pattern that has not been systematically addressed despite UN Security Council Resolution 1325 commitments.

options.A: Pilot the women-mediators initiative in one country and assess feasibility before wider rollout.
options.B: Highlight existing senior women mediators in the programme's storytelling materials.
options.C: Modernise the programme's incident-reporting database for improved analytics.
options.D: Adopt a documented women-mediators initiative funded through the programme, target a minimum 30% women's representation on programme-supported mediation processes, and align with GAP III's commitment to UNSCR 1325 implementation and women, peace, and security objectives in EU external action.

vignette: Ana Soares, Programme Coordinator at a Brussels-based EU-funded human-rights programme, identifies that the programme's small-grants window for civil-society organisations does not currently include a dedicated funding stream for women-led organisations, which represent only 12% of grantees despite their key role in human-rights-defender ecosystems.

options.A: Maintain the current open-application window but add a 'women-led organisations' selection-criterion bonus.
options.B: Renegotiate the programme's secretariat-support contract for cost efficiency.
options.C: Run a high-profile annual 'women human-rights defenders' award.
options.D: Establish a documented dedicated funding stream for women-led civil-society organisations within the small-grants window, target a minimum 40% of grants to women-led organisations by 2027, and align with GAP III's commitment to women's human-rights defenders in EU external action.

vignette: Emma Lambert, EU Delegation Liaison Officer at a Brussels-based foreign-policy think tank, finds that the EU delegation's annual programming review for a key partner country does not currently include a stand-alone gender-equality assessment, despite GAP III's expectation of country-level gender analysis.

options.A: Reference existing gender analyses by other donors in the country and conduct a tailored analysis at the next programming-cycle midpoint.
options.B: Restructure the delegation's contractor-procurement process for improved cost-efficiency.
options.C: Commission a documented country-level gender analysis for the partner country, integrate findings into the next annual programming review, and ensure alignment with GAP III's country-level gender-action-plan expectation, with public disclosure of the analysis methodology.
options.D: Add a paragraph on the delegation's commitment to gender equality to the annual programming review's introduction.

vignette: Stefano Russo, Sector Lead at a Milan-based development-finance institution managing EU-blended-finance facilities for renewable-energy investments in low-income countries, observes that the institution's project-screening criteria do not require demonstrated gender-equality outcomes, despite the EU's gender-equality commitments under GAP III and the European Sustainability Reporting Standards.

options.A: Add gender-equality outcomes as a 'soft' screening consideration rather than a binding requirement, to preserve project-pipeline volume.
options.B: Highlight the institution's existing women-in-renewables investments in marketing materials.
options.C: Renegotiate the institution's regional-office leases for cost efficiency.
options.D: Integrate documented gender-equality outcome requirements into project-screening criteria for blended-finance facilities, target a minimum 60% of facility-supported projects with documented gender-equality outcomes, and align with GAP III's commitment to gender-responsive development finance.

vignette: Beatrice Chen, Cooperation Officer at a Luxembourg-based EU-funded education programme working with Central Asian partner countries, identifies that the programme's higher-education scholarships have a structural gender imbalance — 35% female recipients against an aspirational 50% target, with no documented intervention plan despite five years of data.

options.A: Continue the current selection process and add an aspirational gender-balance target to the programme's communications materials.
options.B: Run a 'women alumni in Europe' showcase celebrating past programme graduates.
options.C: Develop a documented intervention plan to reach the 50% target by 2027, including outreach to women candidates, application-support measures, and country-specific barriers analysis, in alignment with GAP III's commitment to women's access to education in partner countries.
options.D: Restructure the programme's visa-coordination function for improved processing times.

vignette: Hugo Andersson, Senior Programme Adviser at a Stockholm-based bilateral cooperation agency, finds that the agency's strategic-partnership agreements with two partner-country ministries do not currently include explicit gender-equality outcomes tied to programme disbursement — a divergence from GAP III's resource-based-management approach and the agency's published feminist foreign policy.

options.A: Restructure the agency's communications function for improved cost efficiency.
options.B: Renegotiate the strategic-partnership agreements to include documented gender-equality outcomes tied to disbursement schedules, integrate disbursement-trigger gender-equality milestones, and align with both GAP III and the agency's feminist foreign policy framework.
options.C: Issue a public statement on the agency's continuing commitment to feminist foreign policy.
options.D: Maintain the existing partnership agreements unchanged and address the gender-equality dimension through an agreed side-letter referencing GAP III.

vignette: Marta Kowalska, Group Reward Director at a Warsaw-headquartered industrial group with 4,200 employees across Poland, Germany and Czechia, is integrating a recent Czech acquisition into the group reward framework. The acquired entity's Czech payroll shows a 13% raw gender pay gap, but its job-grading methodology is incompatible with the group framework, and the German works council has filed a parallel objection arguing that the integration timeline does not respect the Pay Transparency Directive's joint-pay-assessment obligations. The HR-IT migration vendor has quoted nine months for full pay-equity remediation; the integration MD wants the harmonisation closed in three.

options.A: Commission an external pay-equity benchmark study from a tier-one consultancy and use the findings to refresh the group's compensation philosophy across all jurisdictions.
options.B: Adopt a binding multi-jurisdiction harmonisation plan that maps both legacy job-grading systems onto a single gender-neutral framework before any pay decisions are made, conduct a joint pay assessment in cooperation with the German and Czech works councils as required by Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2023/970, and publish a 12-month remediation schedule with quarterly milestones disclosed in the group's CSRD report.
options.C: Apply a risk-based phased approach — close the largest pay outliers first within the three-month integration window, defer the formal joint pay assessment to the post-integration cycle, and document the rationale as a temporary derogation in the integration playbook.
options.D: Launch a group-wide 'one company, one fair pay' brand initiative tied to the integration milestone, with an internal pledge signed by the integration MD referencing the Pay Transparency Directive.

vignette: Anouk de Vries, VP People at an Amsterdam-based asset manager with 540 employees, identifies that women returning from maternity leave are systematically reassigned to lower-revenue client portfolios and that bonus pools for those portfolios are 38% smaller — a compounding effect that the Pay Transparency Directive's joint-pay-assessment trigger does not isolate cleanly because the raw pay gap of 4.2% sits below the directive's 5% reporting threshold. Two cases are headed to the works council; a third employee has notified an Equal Treatment Commission complaint.

options.A: Issue a formal 'returners' charter' co-signed by the executive committee and works council, citing the Pay Transparency Directive and the Work-Life Balance Directive, and feature returner stories in the firm's quarterly equality update.
options.B: Resolve the three live cases individually through the existing grievance mechanism and add a 'returner sensitivity check' to the next portfolio-allocation cycle, on the grounds that the raw gap remains below the directive threshold.
options.C: Conduct a documented joint pay assessment voluntarily despite the sub-threshold raw gap, as Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2023/970 explicitly permits, address the portfolio-reassignment pattern as detriment under Article 12 of the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158, and remediate both the portfolio assignments and the consequent bonus differentials with retroactive correction over the prior 24 months.
options.D: Roll out a comprehensive coaching programme for all senior portfolio managers focused on inclusive client-portfolio management.

vignette: Marcel Vandeputte, Group HR Director at a Belgian construction conglomerate with 2,800 employees, finds that the group's standard sub-contractor selection criteria allocate 70% of weighting to lowest-bid pricing, which produces a documented pattern of wage suppression at sub-contractor level — disproportionately affecting women cleaners and canteen staff who form 84% of the affected subcontracted workforce. The CSRD value-chain disclosure deadline is in eight months; an investor coalition has flagged the issue.

options.A: Add a gender-equality and decent-work clause to the standard sub-contractor agreement template and publish the updated template on the group's procurement portal as a flagship CSDDD compliance milestone.
options.B: Restructure the sub-contractor selection framework to incorporate documented decent-work and equal-pay-for-work-of-equal-value criteria — including living-wage thresholds and gender-pay-gap reporting at sub-contractor level — in line with the value-chain provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1760 (Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence) and the spirit of Directive (EU) 2023/970, and disclose the methodology under the firm's CSRD value-chain reporting.
options.C: Renegotiate the group's payment-terms framework with all sub-contractors to align with the late-payment directive and improve cash-flow efficiency across the supply chain.
options.D: Apply the new selection criteria prospectively to contracts above €5 million only and treat the legacy contract base as out of scope until the contracts come up for renewal in their natural cycle.

vignette: Sara Bergström, Head of People at a Stockholm-based AI-services firm with 380 employees, has been informed by the firm's algorithmic-recruitment vendor that the system's candidate-ranking model has been trained on the firm's historical hiring data. Internal review shows the vendor's training set encodes a 22% under-selection of female candidates for senior engineering roles, and the procurement contract includes no fairness-audit clause. The firm is also preparing for the AI Act's risk classification deadline.

options.A: Run the system in shadow mode alongside human review for the next two hiring cycles to gather a sufficient evidence base before deciding on more intrusive interventions, and engage the vendor on fairness improvements through the standard quarterly business review.
options.B: Suspend the algorithmic-recruitment system pending a documented fundamental-rights and bias impact assessment under Article 27 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act, which classifies recruitment systems as high-risk under Annex III), renegotiate the vendor contract to include audit rights and bias-mitigation obligations, and integrate the assessment into the firm's broader gender-mainstreaming framework.
options.C: Procure a complementary applicant-tracking system from a different vendor to triangulate candidate evaluation and improve overall recruitment process efficiency.
options.D: Adopt the firm's first 'responsible AI in HR' charter, signed by the CEO, referencing both the AI Act and the EU Gender Equality Strategy, and brief all hiring managers at the next leadership offsite.

vignette: Henrik Sørensen, Compensation Director at a Copenhagen-based financial-services group, faces a Pay Transparency Directive readiness review showing two interlocking issues: the group's discretionary 'merit allocation' adjusts base salary outside the formal pay bands, and 89% of those adjustments have historically gone to men; meanwhile the bonus-deferral policy disproportionately disadvantages parents (predominantly women) who cannot meet the deferral's continuity-of-service requirements. Audit committee escalation is expected.

options.A: Refresh the group's total-rewards branding and employee-value-proposition messaging to better communicate the group's competitive-pay positioning in the Nordic financial-services market.
options.B: Eliminate discretionary 'merit allocation' outside the documented pay bands or place it under the same objective criteria and joint-pay-assessment scrutiny as base pay under Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2023/970, and concurrently revise the bonus-deferral continuity-of-service requirements to comply with Article 11 of the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158 prohibiting detriment based on the exercise of leave rights.
options.C: Tighten governance over the discretionary merit-allocation process by requiring written rationale from line managers and quarterly HR review, while leaving the underlying mechanism intact to preserve managerial flexibility on retention decisions.
options.D: Publish the firm's first stand-alone Pay Transparency Directive readiness report alongside the annual financial accounts, with a CEO foreword acknowledging the historical merit-allocation pattern and a forward commitment to remediation.

vignette: Caterina Rinaldi, HR Director at a Milan-based luxury-goods manufacturer with 1,100 employees, identifies that the firm's piece-rate compensation system in the leather-goods division — used for 280 stitchers and finishers, 92% of whom are women — produces hourly earnings 18% below the firm's male-dominated cutting and quality-control departments doing comparably skilled work. The piece-rate system pre-dates the firm's 2018 collective bargaining agreement; the works council is divided.

options.A: Invest in upgraded ergonomic equipment for the leather-goods division to improve worker health outcomes and overall production efficiency.
options.B: Re-evaluate the piece-rate system using a gender-neutral job-evaluation methodology that documents the equivalence of skill, effort, responsibility and working conditions across the leather-goods, cutting and quality-control departments, regrade the affected roles in line with Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2023/970 on the equal-pay-for-work-of-equal-value principle and Article 157 TFEU, and negotiate the methodology and remediation schedule jointly with the works council.
options.C: Maintain the piece-rate structure in place but introduce a quarterly piece-rate review that adjusts the rate upward when productivity benchmarks are exceeded, with a target of closing 50% of the hourly-earnings gap over five years.
options.D: Restructure the firm's heritage-craft branding to celebrate the leather-goods stitchers and finishers as guardians of the firm's historic craft, including profile features in the firm's corporate communications.

vignette: Andreas Müller, HR Lead at a Munich-based aerospace supplier with 920 employees, learns that the firm's apprenticeship programme — funded jointly by the firm and a German federal grant — has admitted only 12% women over the past five cohorts. Internal review shows the application criteria require a baseline physics qualification that is documented as a barrier to female applicants in regional school data. Meanwhile, two female apprentices have left after pregnancy citing inflexible workshop schedules.

options.A: Replace the physics-qualification screening with a competence-based aptitude assessment validated for indirect-discrimination risk, introduce documented flexible-scheduling protections for pregnant apprentices in line with Council Directive 92/85/EEC and the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158, and report the redesigned programme's gender-balance trajectory in the firm's annual sustainability disclosure.
options.B: Modernise the apprenticeship workshop equipment to align with the firm's Industry 4.0 transformation roadmap.
options.C: Run a 'women in aerospace' national outreach campaign in partnership with the federal grant authority, featuring the firm's apprenticeship programme as a flagship case study at the next industry conference.
options.D: Add a 'women in STEM' nomination route as a parallel pathway to the existing physics-qualification track, capping initial intake at 15% of cohort places to preserve the integrity of the established selection methodology.

vignette: Diogo Almeida, Head of People at a Lisbon-based hospitality group with 1,800 employees across 12 properties, identifies during the Pay Transparency Directive readiness exercise that the group's tip-pooling arrangements vary across properties — at six properties tips are pooled equally, at six others tips accrue individually. Female-dominated housekeeping and back-of-house staff at the six individual-tip properties earn 22% less than peers at the pooled-tip properties for equivalent hours. The general managers regard tip arrangements as a property-level operational matter.

options.A: Recommend tip-pooling as best practice across properties through the next general-managers conference, allow each property to decide on its own implementation timeline, and revisit the topic in 18 months once early adopters have generated operational evidence.
options.B: Publish a group-wide 'fair tips' policy on the careers website and the corporate sustainability portal, signed by the COO, framing tip-pooling as a flagship Pay Transparency Directive readiness commitment.
options.C: Roll out a digital tipping platform across all properties to capture cashless-tip volume more accurately and improve the group's customer-experience analytics.
options.D: Bring tip arrangements within the scope of the group's pay-structures review under Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2023/970, harmonise tip-pooling rules across properties with documented gender-impact analysis, and integrate the harmonised arrangement into the group's collective bargaining negotiations as a non-discretionary element of remuneration.

vignette: Réka Szabó, HR Director at a Budapest-based media group with 670 employees, finds that the group's freelance-contributor pool is structurally bimodal: men receive 71% of the high-paying long-form contracts, women receive 78% of the low-paying short-form and routine-content contracts. The freelance pool falls outside the Pay Transparency Directive's employee-coverage scope but inside the group's CSRD value-chain reporting boundary. A women-journalists collective is preparing a discrimination complaint to the national equal-treatment authority.

options.A: Establish a documented gender-neutral commissioning rubric that ties contract type to deliverable specification rather than commissioner-discretion patterns, audit the past 24 months of commissioning decisions for gender disparity and remediate identified gaps through targeted commissioning, and disclose the rubric and outcomes under the group's CSRD value-chain reporting in line with the Strategy's mainstreaming approach.
options.B: Introduce a soft-target indicator for editors to consider gender balance in their commissioning patterns and commission an external benchmark study against peer publishers before recommending more structural intervention.
options.C: Establish a 'women voices' editorial collective with dedicated commissioning budget and a high-profile launch tied to International Women's Day coverage, framed as a CSRD value-chain initiative.
options.D: Restructure the group's freelance-payment platform to consolidate processing across publications and reduce per-contract administrative cost.

vignette: Kaja Møller, People Director at an Oslo-based offshore-services firm with 740 employees, identifies that the firm's offshore-rotation pattern (28 days on / 28 off) is incompatible with the Work-Life Balance Directive's flexible-working entitlements as transposed into Norwegian law via the EEA framework. Internal data show 11 women have rotated off offshore roles in 18 months citing schedule incompatibility with shared care responsibilities; meanwhile the firm's offshore-pay premium is 42% above onshore equivalents — a structural pay gap that compounds the pattern.

options.A: Launch a 'families offshore' employer-branding programme featuring the firm's commitment to work-life balance for offshore workers, in line with the Work-Life Balance Directive.
options.B: Negotiate improved offshore-medical-evacuation coverage as part of the next round of insurance-broker tenders.
options.C: Maintain the standard 28/28 rotation as the default and grant case-by-case requests for split rotations on a discretionary basis subject to operational feasibility and supervisor approval.
options.D: Pilot a documented split-rotation pattern (14 days on / 14 off) for offshore roles where operationally viable, ring-fence offshore-equivalent pay premiums for split-rotation participants in line with Article 9 of the Work-Life Balance Directive (EU) 2019/1158 (right to request flexible-working arrangements) and the equal-pay principle of Article 157 TFEU, and document the trial methodology and outcomes for industry-wide reference.

vignette: Sigrid Lindqvist, Company Secretary at a Stockholm-listed multinational holding (board: 12 members, currently 33% female non-executive directors), is preparing the firm's response to two simultaneous developments: the 30 June 2026 Women on Boards Directive deadline, and the proposed merger with a smaller listed firm whose board is 8% female. The merger draft term sheet preserves the smaller firm's board representation as a 'cultural continuity' clause; institutional shareholders representing 18% of equity have flagged concerns.

options.A: Issue a joint statement from both boards reaffirming the combined entity's commitment to diversity and inclusion, and announce a flagship 'women in leadership' speaker series sponsored by the merged firm.
options.B: Preserve the 'cultural continuity' clause for an 18-month transition period and commit to align the combined board with the directive at the first natural director-term expiry post-integration, documenting the deferral as an exceptional integration measure.
options.C: Strengthen the merger's governance package by adding two new independent non-executive directors with deep sector expertise to the combined-entity board.
options.D: Renegotiate the merger term sheet to require the combined-entity board to meet the 40% target for non-executive directors of the under-represented sex by 30 June 2026 in line with Article 5 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381, replace the 'cultural continuity' clause with a documented integration-plan committee tasked with combined-entity director recruitment under Article 4, and disclose the trajectory to the merger-approval shareholder meeting.

vignette: Tomáš Procházka, Head of Group Governance at a Prague-listed industrial group, observes that while the group's main-board non-executive director composition is at 38% (one short of the directive target with one director term expiring in 2026), the group's three operating-subsidiary boards — through which actual operational power flows — have between 0 and 18% female non-executive directors. The directive applies to the listed parent entity only, but the group's CSRD report consolidates governance disclosure across the structure.

options.A: Publish a group-wide governance charter committing to the spirit of the Women on Boards Directive across all subsidiaries, with a section in the integrated annual report explicitly mapping subsidiary-level practice against the directive's articles.
options.B: Prioritise main-board compliance with the directive deadline as the binding regulatory obligation, and maintain subsidiary-board composition decisions at the discretion of the operating-CEO of each subsidiary subject to a high-level group governance code.
options.C: Commission an independent review of the group's subsidiary-board governance effectiveness, with recommendations on board-meeting frequency and committee structures.
options.D: Extend the spirit of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 voluntarily to the operating-subsidiary boards by adopting documented gender-balance targets at subsidiary level mirroring the 40% non-executive-director target, integrate the subsidiary-level targets into the group's CSRD ESRS S1 disclosure on workforce diversity, and replace the upcoming main-board vacancy with a director of the under-represented sex to clear the directive threshold.

vignette: Aurélie Fontaine, Chief of Staff at a Paris-listed insurance group, finds two compounding issues ahead of the 2026 Women on Boards deadline: the existing nominations committee has interpreted Article 5 of the directive as permitting a 'qualifications equivalence' override that has been used to justify three consecutive male appointments since 2023, and a recent governance review concluded that this interpretation does not survive scrutiny. The CEO has signalled support for a fourth such appointment at the next AGM.

options.A: Reject the proposed fourth appointment, issue an updated nominations-committee charter clarifying that Article 5(2) of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 requires equal-qualification candidates of the under-represented sex to be given priority, document the appointment rationale for every director nomination going forward as required by Article 5(4), and disclose the corrected interpretation in the corporate governance statement.
options.B: Strengthen the nominations committee's independence by introducing a rotating external advisor with deep insurance-sector expertise.
options.C: Proceed with the proposed appointment under the existing interpretation, given that the legal opinion underpinning the override has not been formally challenged, and revisit the nominations-committee charter at the next governance-review cycle.
options.D: Publish a board-diversity policy aligned with the firm's signed commitment to the 30% Club, and feature the chair of the nominations committee in an investor-relations video on the firm's commitment to the Women on Boards Directive.

vignette: Mohammad Al-Rashid, Head of Talent at a Dublin-listed pharmaceutical group, finds that while the main board has reached 41% female representation, the executive committee remains 11% female (1 of 9), and the natural successor pool to the executive committee — 28 director-1 leaders — is 14% female. A shareholder ESG-engagement letter has cited the inversion as evidence that Women on Boards Directive compliance is being treated cosmetically.

options.A: Reorganise the executive committee's portfolios to better align with the firm's three-year strategic plan and improve cross-functional decision-making.
options.B: Launch a high-profile 'women lead pharma' initiative including external advisory-board appointments for senior women in the director-1 cohort and a flagship internal mentoring programme paired with the existing chair-and-CEO sponsorship.
options.C: Establish a documented executive-committee succession plan with a target of at least 40% representation of the under-represented sex by 2028 mirroring the Women on Boards Directive trajectory at executive level, undertake a structured pipeline-development programme for senior women in the director-1 cohort, and disclose succession-pipeline gender data annually under ESRS S1 of the firm's CSRD report.
options.D: Commit to fill the next two executive-committee vacancies with female candidates as natural attrition occurs, while preserving the existing succession-planning approach for other roles to avoid disruption.

vignette: Eleni Christou, Head of People at an Athens-listed retail group with 6,200 employees, reviews two concurrent issues: the firm's franchise network — 340 outlets — operates under a 'preferred manager profile' template that has produced a 22% female franchise-manager rate against a 51% female applicant rate, and the firm's directly-owned-store regional-manager appointments have an 18% female rate against a 49% female store-manager candidate pool. The directive's 40% target applies to listed-parent-board level; the franchise and regional-manager populations sit below the threshold but inside the firm's broader gender-mainstreaming commitments.

options.A: Migrate the franchise-management software to a unified national platform to improve operational visibility across the network.
options.B: Showcase the firm's small but high-performing cohort of female regional managers and franchise managers in the firm's investor day and on the group's careers website.
options.C: Apply the new selection rubric prospectively to vacant positions as they arise through natural attrition and refresh the 'preferred manager profile' template to remove the most overtly stereotyped language.
options.D: Replace the 'preferred manager profile' template with a documented competence-based selection rubric mirroring the spirit of Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 (gender-neutral selection criteria) at all leadership tiers below board level, audit the past three years' franchise and regional-manager appointments for unexplained variance, and integrate the leadership-pipeline gender data into the firm's annual sustainability statement.

vignette: Lukas Brandt, Head of Corporate Governance at a Vienna-listed energy utility, identifies that the supervisory board has reached 40% female representation but the management board (Vorstand) — which under Austrian dual-board governance carries primary executive authority — is 0% female (4 men), and that the Austrian transposition of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 explicitly applies to the supervisory board but is silent on the management board. Three management-board terms expire over the next 24 months.

options.A: Treat the supervisory-board 40% achievement as the binding directive-level outcome and address management-board composition through natural attrition and the existing succession-planning process led by the supervisory board.
options.B: Adopt a documented management-board renewal plan targeting at least 40% representation of the under-represented sex on the management board by the conclusion of the three upcoming term expiries, in alignment with the spirit and the textual recital of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 and the EU Strategy's commitment to gender-balanced leadership in management positions, and disclose the trajectory in the corporate governance statement.
options.C: Issue a joint statement from the supervisory and management boards committing to gender-balanced leadership across both governance tiers, framed against the Strategy's 50%-by-2024 internal benchmark for the European Commission's own management.
options.D: Restructure the supervisory-board committee architecture to introduce a dedicated transition committee aligned with the firm's energy-transition strategy.

vignette: Patrik Novák, Group HR Director at a Bratislava-listed IT-services firm operating in Slovakia, Czechia and Romania, finds that the firm has used the directive's alternative reporting regime under Article 6 (procedural-only) for the past two reporting cycles, and that the chosen procedural targets — gender-balanced shortlists, structured interviews — have been formally adopted but not implemented in practice (audit shows zero gender-balanced shortlists submitted in the past 12 months). The 30 June 2026 substantive deadline approaches; the chairman's preferred course is to remain on the procedural regime.

options.A: Switch from the Article 6 procedural-only regime to substantive 40% target compliance under Article 5 of Directive (EU) 2022/2381, given the documented implementation gap, develop a board-renewal plan to reach the 40% target by 30 June 2026 with quarterly progress reporting to the nominations committee, and disclose both the regime change and the underlying implementation gap in the corporate governance statement.
options.B: Maintain the procedural-only regime, tighten the nominations-committee secretariat's enforcement of the gender-balanced-shortlist requirement, and treat the implementation gap as a process-control issue to be addressed through the existing internal-audit cycle.
options.C: Strengthen the existing Article 6 procedural commitments by adding additional reporting metrics and external auditor attestation of the procedural compliance, retaining the procedural-only regime as the firm's chosen pathway.
options.D: Refresh the corporate-governance code to align with recent updates to the Slovak Corporate Governance Code published by the Bratislava Stock Exchange.

vignette: Catherine O'Brien, Head of Group Strategy at a Dublin-listed financial-services group, observes that the group's executive-development programme — which feeds the C-suite — has admitted 23% women over five cohorts, that the women admitted have an 81% completion rate against a 68% male completion rate, but that women's post-completion C-suite placement rate is 11% against a 43% male rate. Internal HR data attribute the placement gap to informal sponsor-allocation patterns at executive-committee level.

options.A: Run a high-profile 'women in finance leadership' alumni network anchored in the executive-development programme, with quarterly events featuring the firm's C-suite as keynote sponsors.
options.B: Restructure the executive-development programme to include a documented post-completion sponsorship-allocation protocol with gender-balanced sponsor-mentee matching at executive-committee level, target a placement-rate parity by 2027 mirroring the European Commission's 50% gender-balance internal target, and disclose programme outcomes by gender in the firm's annual sustainability statement under ESRS S1.
options.C: Strengthen mentor-allocation transparency by introducing an opt-in registry of senior leaders willing to sponsor under-represented talent, allowing senior leaders to nominate themselves and be matched on a voluntary basis.
options.D: Extend the executive-development programme from 12 to 18 months to allow deeper engagement with the strategic-leadership content modules.

vignette: Magdalena Walczak, Chief of Staff at a Warsaw-listed retail group, identifies a pattern across 11 listed firms in the same sector: all 11 boards are converging on Article 5 substantive compliance via a small pool of approximately 40 'professional non-executive directors' of the under-represented sex, who hold an average of 2.3 directorships each. The firm's nominations committee has identified one such director for the next vacancy. The director's existing portfolio is at the conventional boundary of board commitment.

options.A: Add a sector-expertise criterion to the director-skills matrix to better align future appointments with the firm's three-year strategic priorities.
options.B: Proceed with the proposed appointment given the candidate's qualifications and ready availability, and refresh the search-firm panel for the next vacancy to bring in alternative candidate sources at that point in the cycle.
options.C: Decline the proposed appointment in favour of a documented broadened director-search process that surfaces candidates from sources beyond the existing 'professional non-executive director' pool — including operational-leadership candidates, sector-adjacent expertise, and candidates from below the conventional board-experience threshold — in line with Article 4(2)(b) of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 (selection based on a comparative analysis of qualifications) rather than reliance on a narrow existing-director pool.
options.D: Proceed with the proposed appointment and concurrently announce a long-term commitment to broadening the director-pipeline through a partnership with a women-on-boards executive-search firm, framed as a multi-year directive-readiness investment.

vignette: Jakob Hansen, Company Secretary at a Copenhagen-listed shipping group, faces a tactical dilemma: the firm's board has 36% female non-executive directors with one upcoming vacancy. The chairman favours appointing a male candidate with deep maritime-regulatory expertise (highly relevant to the firm's strategic priorities), arguing that the remaining vacancy after this one will allow easy compliance with Article 5 by the directive deadline. The nominations-committee minority has objected that this tactical approach inverts the directive's intent.

options.A: Strengthen the firm's regulatory-affairs function by hiring a dedicated head of maritime regulation reporting to the CEO.
options.B: Decline the chairman's proposed appointment for the current vacancy, document the rationale by reference to Article 4(2) of Directive (EU) 2022/2381 (gender-neutral selection at every appointment, not aggregated across a sequence), and develop a director-skills matrix for the joint vacancies that allows maritime-regulatory expertise to be sourced from a candidate of the under-represented sex.
options.C: Adopt a board-diversity charter committing to the spirit of the directive at every individual appointment decision, present the charter at the next AGM, and feature it on the firm's investor-relations site.
options.D: Proceed with the proposed appointment given the strategic relevance of maritime-regulatory expertise, document the rationale comprehensively per Article 5(4), and commit to filling the next vacancy with a candidate of the under-represented sex to ensure overall directive compliance by the deadline.

vignette: Margit Kovács, Head of Compliance at a Budapest-headquartered telecom group with 4,800 employees across Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria, faces a complex case: a senior male engineering director is the subject of seven harassment complaints from three female reports across two countries. The Hungarian transposition of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 is operative; the Romanian transposition delays the cyberviolence provisions; the Bulgarian transposition is incomplete. The firm's internal harassment policy is the same across all three jurisdictions. One complainant has been informally encouraged by HR to accept a performance-based settlement.

options.A: Apply the local-jurisdiction standard separately in each country, accept the performance-based settlement where the local jurisdiction permits it, and revisit the group-wide harassment policy at the next compliance-cycle review once all three jurisdictions have completed transposition.
options.B: Apply the firm's internal harassment policy at the highest of the applicable national standards across all three jurisdictions, withdraw any settlement offer that includes confidentiality clauses on the underlying conduct (which Article 19 of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 requires Member States to prohibit), open a formal investigation suspending the engineering director's line authority pending outcome, and engage trauma-informed support for the complainants in alignment with the Victims' Rights Directive 2012/29/EU.
options.C: Roll out a group-wide manager-effectiveness training programme covering performance management, feedback, and team development.
options.D: Issue a group-wide leadership-conduct statement co-signed by the CEOs of the three national operating companies referencing Directive (EU) 2024/1385 and the firm's commitment to the Istanbul Convention values.

vignette: Cristina Ferreira, Compliance Officer at a Porto-based construction group with 2,100 directly-employed staff and 6,400 sub-contractor workers across 18 sites, has received a complaint that female sub-contractor cleaners on three sites are subject to sustained sexual harassment by sub-contractor supervisors. The firm's harassment policy applies only to directly-employed staff; the sub-contractor agreements include a generic 'compliance with applicable law' clause but no specific harassment-protection obligation. Under EU jurisprudence the firm may be jointly liable.

options.A: Encourage sub-contractors to adopt their own harassment policies through a non-binding principles document attached to existing sub-contractor agreements, and establish a quarterly review of sub-contractor reports of harassment as part of the firm's vendor-management process.
options.B: Extend the firm's harassment policy to cover all sub-contractor workers on firm-controlled sites in alignment with Article 7 of ILO Convention No. 190 (which covers all workers regardless of contractual status), introduce an immediate independent-investigation channel for sub-contractor harassment reports, embed harassment-protection obligations into the sub-contractor agreement template, and document the chain of responsibility under the value-chain due-diligence provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1760.
options.C: Strengthen the firm's site-safety equipment standards including PPE specifications, fall-protection systems, and CCTV coverage of high-risk operational areas.
options.D: Issue a public commitment to ILO Convention No. 190 standards across the firm's full value chain and announce a flagship 'safe sites' initiative for International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women.

vignette: Lina Sandberg, Trust & Safety Lead at a Gothenburg-based social-media platform with 2.4 million EU users, reviews two compounding signals: female users report receiving online sexual harassment at 4× the rate of male users, and the platform's automated content-moderation system has a 31% false-negative rate on intimate-image abuse reports involving women, against an 8% false-negative rate on equivalent reports involving men. The platform is preparing for both Directive (EU) 2024/1385 transposition deadlines and the DSA Article 35 risk-mitigation obligations.

options.A: Conduct a documented risk assessment of the content-moderation system's gendered false-negative rate as a high-risk AI system under Annex III of the AI Act, retrain the moderation model with a balanced training corpus and human-review escalation for intimate-image abuse cases, integrate the mitigation into the DSA Article 35 systemic-risk-mitigation report, and align with the cyberviolence provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 and the platform's Article 27 fundamental-rights impact assessment.
options.B: Tighten the moderation-rule thresholds for intimate-image abuse cases and increase human-review capacity by 15% in the moderation operations centre, treating the false-negative differential as a calibration issue addressable through operational adjustment.
options.C: Roll out new user-interface features that improve the discoverability of the platform's existing reporting tools and reduce friction in the user-report submission flow.
options.D: Publish a transparency report on the platform's commitment to combating gender-based online violence, anchored in the platform's signing of the EU Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online.

vignette: Henrik Johansen, HR Director at an Oslo-based offshore-vessel-operations firm with 380 employees, learns that female cadets on long offshore deployments report a pattern of 'normalised banter' that escalates to sexual harassment in approximately 22% of deployments. The firm's grievance procedure exists but requires reports to be made to the vessel master — frequently the perpetrator's social peer or implicit superior. Two cadets have left the cadet programme citing the dynamic; the maritime cadetship-funding authority has flagged the gender-attrition pattern.

options.A: Upgrade the cadet welfare offering with improved on-vessel internet connectivity, recreation facilities, and shore-leave allocation.
options.B: Run a 'respect at sea' fleet-wide campaign featuring ship-masters and cadets in posters and digital communications, anchored in the firm's commitment to the IMO Maritime Labour Convention and the Strategy's commitment to challenging stereotypes.
options.C: Tighten the existing grievance procedure with a dual-route option allowing reports to be made either to the vessel master or to the shore-side HR contact at the cadet's discretion, supported by enhanced master-effectiveness training.
options.D: Establish a documented offshore-harassment reporting protocol that bypasses the vessel-master line, including a confidential 24/7 shore-based reporting channel, mandatory shore-side investigation for reports involving senior crew, and immediate cadet-rotation-out protections, in alignment with Article 10 of ILO Convention No. 190 (on safe and effective dispute mechanisms) and the third-party-violence provisions, alongside the ISM Code's safety-management framework.

vignette: Federica Ricci, Director of Marketing at a Florence-based fashion retailer with 1,400 employees, faces an internal-stakeholder split over the firm's autumn campaign creative brief, which features sexualised imagery of young female models in workplace office settings. Internal HR data show the firm has had 14 sexual-harassment incidents in the past 24 months; an internal employee survey links the imagery to a 'culture of objectification.' The chief creative officer argues the imagery is a heritage element of the brand identity and central to the campaign's commercial performance.

options.A: Veto the campaign creative in its current form, adopt a documented marketing-content review policy aligned with the EU Strategy's commitment to challenging gender stereotypes in advertising and the AVMSD's Article 6 on respect for human dignity in commercial communications, integrate the policy into creative-brief sign-off as a binding gate, and brief the chief creative officer that the policy applies equally to heritage-brand and new-campaign work.
options.B: Add a new 'women in leadership at the brand' editorial track to the autumn campaign alongside the existing creative direction, increasing the visibility of senior female executives in the campaign's overall communications mix.
options.C: Soften the most overtly sexualised elements of the autumn campaign creative through a tonal adjustment in collaboration with the chief creative officer, and revisit the broader marketing-content review policy at the next brand-strategy refresh in 18 months.
options.D: Refocus the campaign's media-mix strategy to emphasise direct-to-consumer digital channels and reduce dependence on traditional out-of-home advertising.

vignette: Kai Brandt, Head of People at a Berlin-headquartered pharmaceutical group with 5,400 employees across the EU, faces a recurrent issue: senior male sales representatives in the German and Italian markets habitually entertain customers — predominantly male physicians — at venues where female sales representatives report repeated sexual-harassment incidents involving customers. The German works council has demanded action; the Italian operating company's general manager regards the practice as a commercial necessity. A female sales representative has just resigned citing the dynamic.

options.A: Reinforce the firm's existing code of conduct with a stand-alone customer-entertainment annex referencing ILO Convention No. 190 and Directive (EU) 2024/1385, signed by the regional sales directors as a leadership commitment.
options.B: Issue a documented group-wide customer-entertainment policy requiring all firm-organised customer events to take place at venues meeting documented respect-and-safety standards, ban firm-sponsored attendance at venues with documented harassment risk, extend the firm's harassment policy to cover firm-sanctioned customer-entertainment activities in alignment with ILO Convention No. 190's broad workplace definition, and engage the works councils in both markets on policy implementation.
options.C: Roll out an updated group-wide customer-relationship-management platform to improve sales-pipeline analytics across both markets.
options.D: Issue venue-selection guidance through the firm's commercial-operations function, recommending that customer events take place at venues without documented harassment risk, while preserving sales-representative discretion on customer-entertainment formats and frequency.

vignette: Anneli Korhonen, Head of Editorial at a Helsinki-based public-broadcasting service, faces two interlocking issues following the broadcaster's investigative coverage of organised-crime networks: three female reporters have received targeted online-harassment campaigns including doxxing, deepfake intimate-image generation, and coordinated platform reporting that has led to temporary social-media suspension. Internal HR has framed it as an 'editorial-risk' matter; the broadcaster's safety-of-journalists protocol does not address gendered online violence.

options.A: Issue an editorial-leadership statement condemning the harassment of journalists, anchored in the broadcaster's signing of the Council of Europe Recommendation on the protection of journalism and the safety of journalists.
options.B: Strengthen the broadcaster's overall editorial cybersecurity posture by procuring an upgraded enterprise endpoint-protection platform.
options.C: Update the safety-of-journalists protocol to address gendered online violence as a distinct workplace-safety issue, including legal-support coverage, platform-takedown coordination via Article 16 of the DSA, deepfake-removal procedures under Article 5 of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 (criminalisation of non-consensual intimate-image dissemination including deepfakes), and trauma-informed counselling provision aligned with the Victims' Rights Directive 2012/29/EU.
options.D: Activate the existing safety-of-journalists protocol on a case-by-case basis for the three live cases, and review the gender dimension of the protocol at the next annual safety-protocol refresh cycle.

vignette: Eva Janssen, HR Director at an Amsterdam-based hospital group with 11,800 employees, identifies that 38% of female nursing staff report having experienced patient-perpetrated sexual harassment in the past 12 months, that 92% of those incidents went unreported through formal channels (most commonly because nurses regarded the dynamic as a 'normal part of the job'), and that the hospital group's existing patient-incident reporting workflow categorises such events under 'patient agitation' alongside non-gendered behaviours. A nurse-union ballot is imminent.

options.A: Add a 'gender-based agitation' sub-category to the existing patient-incident workflow and provide additional training to ward managers on de-escalation techniques for incidents involving sexual content.
options.B: Refurbish patient-facing nursing stations across the group to improve the work environment and reduce nurse fatigue.
options.C: Reclassify patient-perpetrated sexual harassment as a distinct workplace-safety incident category in the patient-incident reporting workflow, introduce a documented response protocol including trained shore-side patient-incident response leads, post-incident counselling, and an explicit nurse-led right to refuse to provide non-emergency care to patients with documented incidents, in alignment with Article 7 of ILO Convention No. 190 and the workplace-safety provisions of EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC.
options.D: Run a hospital-wide 'respect for caregivers' communications campaign anchored in the hospital group's commitment to ILO Convention No. 190, with messaging displayed in patient-facing areas.

vignette: Tomáš Bednár, Head of HR at a Prague-based logistics firm with 1,650 employees, learns that the firm's third-party recruitment vendor — used for high-volume warehouse hiring — has been screening female applicants for marital status and pregnancy intentions in informal pre-screening calls, a practice that violates EU equal-treatment law. The vendor's contract is on auto-renewal in 60 days. The HR director defends the vendor's commercial track record.

options.A: Suspend the vendor's pre-screening operations pending a documented compliance investigation, terminate the vendor contract for material breach if the investigation confirms the practice, replace the vendor's pre-screening process with a documented competence-only screening framework consistent with Article 4 of Directive 2006/54/EC (the Recast Equal Treatment Directive) and Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2023/970 (gender-neutral selection criteria), and report the incident to the firm's compliance committee and external auditor.
options.B: Issue a formal warning to the vendor with a 90-day cure period, monitor the vendor's screening practices through the next two recruitment cycles before deciding on contract continuation, and document the case in the firm's vendor-risk register.
options.C: Migrate the firm's applicant-tracking system to a more efficient cloud platform with stronger candidate-pipeline analytics.
options.D: Issue an updated vendor code of conduct referencing Directive 2006/54/EC and the Pay Transparency Directive, require all third-party recruitment vendors to sign the updated code, and feature the code on the firm's procurement portal.

vignette: Helena Nilsson, Head of Communications at a Malmö-based EU-funded media-literacy NGO, has been asked by the NGO's largest donor to deliver a campaign on online violence against women, but the donor's strategic communications team has insisted on a creative direction that emphasises individual women's resilience and self-protection strategies, rather than the structural and platform-accountability dimensions covered by Directive (EU) 2024/1385. The donor framing is endorsed by the NGO's communications director on engagement-metric grounds.

options.A: Deliver the campaign in the donor's preferred creative direction with strong production values, framing the emphasis on individual resilience as a complementary track to the NGO's broader policy-advocacy work.
options.B: Compromise on a hybrid campaign creative — 70% individual-resilience messaging per donor preference, 30% structural-intervention messaging consistent with the NGO's policy stance — and document the trade-off in the campaign-evaluation report.
options.C: Renegotiate the campaign creative direction with the donor to centre platform-accountability, structural intervention, and the criminalisation provisions of Directive (EU) 2024/1385 (Articles 5 to 9) alongside victim-resilience messaging, ground the campaign in the Victims' Rights Directive 2012/29/EU's framing of victims' rights, and document the methodology in alignment with the EU Strategy's commitment to addressing online violence as a structural rather than individual issue.
options.D: Strengthen the NGO's general media-monitoring capability by procuring an upgraded media-intelligence platform.

vignette: Marta Vukmir, Head of People Analytics at a Zagreb-based banking group with 3,400 employees across Croatia, Slovenia and Bosnia, is preparing the group's CSRD-aligned ESRS S1 disclosure on workforce diversity. The current disclosure plan reports on gender alone; senior leadership has resisted disaggregation by ethnicity (Roma, Bosniak, Albanian minorities) and disability on cost grounds. Internal grievance data show that Roma women face documented compounding barriers in branch-level promotion, an effect invisible in single-axis reporting.

options.A: Modernise the bank's HR-information-system to consolidate workforce reporting across the three jurisdictions on a single cloud platform.
options.B: Continue gender-only disaggregation for the current CSRD reporting cycle and commission an internal feasibility study on intersectional data collection to inform the next reporting cycle's scope.
options.C: Add an 'intersectionality' narrative section to the ESRS S1 disclosure referencing the Strategy's intersectional framework and the firm's commitment to the Council of Europe Roma Strategic Action Plan.
options.D: Adopt intersectional disaggregation (gender × ethnicity × disability) in the ESRS S1 disclosure, introduce safeguarded data-collection protocols compliant with GDPR Article 9 (special-category data), align the disclosure framework with the Strategy's intersectional commitment to addressing the situation of Roma women specifically, and integrate the intersectional findings into the bank's anti-discrimination training and branch-management performance review.

vignette: Elena Rusu, Sustainability Director at a Bucharest-based industrial conglomerate, faces a layered CSRD challenge: the firm's value chain includes Bangladeshi garment suppliers (predominantly women workers), Romanian agricultural seasonal labour (mixed migrant workforce), and Italian luxury sub-contractors (older female craftworkers). The current ESRS-aligned materiality assessment treats gender equality as a workforce metric for direct employees only. The CSDDD transposition deadline approaches.

options.A: Expand the materiality assessment to include intersectional gender-equality impacts across the full value chain — gender × migration status (Bangladeshi/Romanian agricultural), gender × age (Italian craftworkers), gender × ethnicity — integrate findings into supplier-engagement protocols, and disclose results under the value-chain reporting requirements of the CSRD ESRS S2 standard and the human-rights due-diligence obligations of Directive (EU) 2024/1760.
options.B: Add a section to the firm's sustainability report on the firm's commitment to women across the value chain, anchored in the Strategy's mainstreaming framework and the firm's signing of the UN Women's Empowerment Principles.
options.C: Renegotiate logistics contracts across the value chain to reduce supply-chain carbon emissions in line with the firm's net-zero commitment.
options.D: Apply value-chain gender-equality assessment to tier-1 suppliers only in the current reporting cycle and extend coverage to lower tiers progressively as the firm's CSDDD compliance roadmap matures.

vignette: Jens Mortensen, Strategic Planning Lead at a Copenhagen-based regional health agency, is reviewing two policy strands due for refresh: the cardiovascular-care pathway (which published evidence shows under-recognises symptoms in women, particularly older women and women of South Asian descent) and the perinatal mental-health pathway (which under-serves teen mothers and migrant mothers). The agency's current quality-improvement framework treats gender, age, and ethnicity as separate streams; the chief clinical officer is sceptical of integrated intersectional design.

options.A: Publish a thematic public-health report on women's cardiovascular and perinatal mental health, anchored in the Strategy and the EU's Cancer Plan, with launch event featuring senior clinical leaders.
options.B: Procure a new electronic-health-record system to streamline clinical documentation and improve overall care-pathway analytics.
options.C: Restructure both pathways using an intersectional design framework (gender × age × ethnicity) with documented disaggregated outcome metrics, train clinical staff on intersectional symptom recognition, integrate the outcome data into the agency's quality-improvement cycle, and align the redesign with the Strategy's gender-mainstreaming approach to health policy and the EU's commitment to the Beijing Platform for Action's intersectional health framework.
options.D: Pilot intersectional disaggregation in the cardiovascular pathway in the current quality cycle, evaluate the operational feasibility, and extend to the perinatal mental-health pathway only in the next cycle if the pilot produces actionable findings.

vignette: Anna Lehtinen, Programme Director at a Helsinki-based vocational-training agency, faces a structural challenge: the agency's reskilling programmes for the green transition target unemployed workers from carbon-intensive industries, but the participant pool is 91% male, reflecting the gender composition of the affected industries. The agency's ESF+ funding documentation requires gender-mainstreaming evidence. The carbon-intensive-industry attrition pattern affects women in adjacent sectors (administration, logistics) who are not currently in the agency's outreach scope.

options.A: Modernise the agency's online-learning platform to support hybrid programme delivery and reduce delivery costs.
options.B: Run a 'women in the green transition' targeted recruitment campaign anchored in the Strategy's gender-mainstreaming approach to the European Green Deal, with high-profile events and partnership with women's-employment NGOs.
options.C: Expand the agency's reskilling-programme outreach scope to include workers in adjacent sectors affected by carbon-intensive-industry decline (administration, logistics, retail) where women are concentrated, redesign programme-eligibility criteria with intersectional consideration of gender × age × prior-sector, and integrate the redesign into the agency's ESF+ gender-mainstreaming compliance and the Just Transition Fund's gender-equality provisions under Regulation (EU) 2021/1056.
options.D: Maintain the existing eligibility criteria centred on direct carbon-intensive-industry workers, supplement with a small dedicated 'women in the transition' track funded outside the main ESF+ programme to demonstrate gender-mainstreaming intent.

vignette: Kornelia Müller, Head of Procurement at a Vienna-based federal-state authority, is overseeing the procurement of an AI-driven welfare-eligibility-assessment system covering 280,000 beneficiaries. Vendor proposals show a 14-percentage-point gendered false-rejection differential, with a further intersectional differential affecting women with disabilities (additional 9 points) and migrant women (additional 11 points). The procurement is at the final-evaluation stage. The lead vendor's mitigation plan addresses the gender axis only.

options.A: Proceed with the lead vendor selection on the basis of the gender-axis mitigation plan, require post-deployment monitoring on the disability and migration axes through the existing vendor-management process, and revisit the intersectional procurement requirement at the next AI-Act-readiness review.
options.B: Strengthen the authority's broader procurement-governance framework by introducing a unified vendor-risk register across all departments.
options.C: Add a dedicated intersectional-fairness clause to the procurement framework anchored in the AI Act and the EU Disability Rights Strategy, and feature the requirement in the authority's published procurement principles.
options.D: Reject the lead vendor's proposal in its current form, require all final-stage vendors to submit a documented intersectional fundamental-rights impact assessment under Article 27 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act, classifying welfare-eligibility-assessment systems as high-risk under Annex III), specifying mitigation across the gender, disability and migration axes, and integrate the requirement into the procurement framework alongside the Strategy's intersectional gender-mainstreaming commitments.

vignette: Bartosz Lewandowski, Innovation Director at a Wrocław-based smart-city public-private partnership, faces a tension: the partnership's predictive-policing pilot has produced documented over-targeting of Roma women in three districts, an intersectional pattern not visible in the original gender-mainstreaming impact assessment which considered only the gender axis. The municipal stakeholders have flagged civil-society objections; the policing partner argues the pilot's overall accuracy metrics justify continuation.

options.A: Recalibrate the predictive-policing model with additional Roma-specific training data to address the over-targeting issue, maintain the pilot's operational continuity through the recalibration period, and report progress to the municipal stakeholders.
options.B: Procure additional municipal-data infrastructure to improve the predictive-policing pilot's data-availability footprint.
options.C: Suspend the predictive-policing pilot pending a documented intersectional fundamental-rights impact assessment under Article 27 of the AI Act (predictive-policing being explicitly prohibited or high-risk under Annex II–III depending on use case), engage civil-society representatives in the redesign, and align with both the Strategy's intersectional gender-mainstreaming framework and the Council of Europe Roma Strategic Action Plan.
options.D: Issue a partnership statement on intersectional algorithmic fairness anchored in the AI Act and the EU's commitment to the Roma Strategic Action Plan, and feature the statement at the next smart-city forum.

vignette: Sandra Costa, Sustainability Officer at a Lisbon-based luxury-tourism group with 3,200 employees and a 14,000-strong supplier base across Portugal, Cape Verde and São Tomé, has identified that the group's CSRD value-chain disclosure must address an intersectional pattern: women in the group's African supplier base face compounding climate-vulnerability and economic-vulnerability that an apparently gender-neutral climate-resilience programme funded by the group does not reach because the programme operates through male village-head intermediaries. The CSRD reporting deadline is in 11 months.

options.A: Add a 'women's voice' advisory mechanism to the existing climate-resilience programme structure, retaining the male-intermediary delivery channel for operational continuity, and evaluate the advisory mechanism's effectiveness over a 24-month horizon.
options.B: Add a featured chapter on the climate-resilience programme to the group's annual sustainability report, anchored in the firm's commitment to the UN Women's Empowerment Principles and the Sustainable Development Goal 5.
options.C: Restructure the climate-resilience programme to operate through women's-cooperative intermediaries with documented gender-disaggregated outcome metrics, integrate the redesign into the group's CSRD ESRS E1 (climate) and S2 (value chain) disclosures, and align the methodology with both the Gender Action Plan III's gender-responsive climate-action framework and the Strategy's intersectional commitment to the situation of women in low-income partner countries.
options.D: Renegotiate the group's tour-operator contracts in the African supplier base to improve commercial terms and customer-experience alignment.

vignette: Maria Schmidt, Head of EU Affairs at a Brussels-based industry association representing 220 member firms across the construction sector, is coordinating the association's response to the Commission's revision of the Construction Products Regulation. The current draft response emphasises competitiveness and red-tape concerns; the gender dimension of construction-product safety (e.g. PPE design defaulting to male-body specifications, with documented injury implications for female construction workers) is absent from the draft. Two member firms have flagged the omission privately.

options.A: Expand the association's consultation response to include the gender dimension of construction-product safety — including PPE design specifications that account for female-body morphology — anchor the response in Article 8 TFEU (gender-mainstreaming clause), the Strategy's commitment to addressing gender-blind product design, and the spirit of EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC on workplace safety, and request that the revised regulation include gender-disaggregated injury-data reporting requirements.
options.B: Schedule a high-profile association event on construction-sector competitiveness and innovation to coincide with the consultation deadline.
options.C: Add a brief paragraph to the consultation response affirming the association's commitment to gender-equal workplace safety and citing the Strategy.
options.D: Submit the existing draft on the regulatory-burden grounds prioritised by member firms, and address the gender dimension through a follow-up bilateral engagement with the responsible Commission Directorate-General.

vignette: Eleni Vasilakis, Programme Director at a Thessaloniki-based EU-funded migration-integration programme, faces an intersectional challenge: the programme's standard intervention package — language training, employment placement, social-services referral — is poorly accessed by women migrants from third countries with documented patterns of restricted-mobility, intra-household decision-making constraints, and gender-based-violence vulnerability. Programme outcome data show 11% female participation against a regional female-migrant population of 47%. The next funding cycle is being designed.
